revision.comment: Auto generating page. date placed far in the past so it is attributed to Immanuelle and FireflyBot sends them messages if it is ever imported to Wikipedia.

revision.comment: Programmatically generating so that if this gets imported to wikipedia as a draft, the bot will attribute it to Immanuelle and send them g6 warnings

[[File:Ōmuroyama Sengen Jinja Torii.jpg|thumb|the entrance torii]] Omuroyama Sengen Shrine is a Shrine for [[Iwanaga-hime]]
It has a wikimedia commons category here [https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/Category%3A%C5%8Cmuroyama_Sengen_Jinja]
Here's a structured version of the galleries with subheadings and detailed captions:
---
## Hie Shrine
### Main Shrine (Honden) <gallery mode="packed"> File:Hie Shrine top.jpg|alt=The main hall of Hie Shrine.|Honden - The main hall of Hie Shrine, a place of worship and cultural significance. </gallery>
### Escalators <gallery mode="packed"> File:Hie Shrine Escalator view.jpg|alt=Escalators leading up to the shrine.|Escalators from the top - A unique feature providing convenient access to the shrine. File:Escalator at Hie Shrine.jpg|alt=Escalators leading up to the shrine.|Escalators from the top - Another view of the escalators. File:Hie Shrine Escalator.jpg|alt=Escalators leading up to the shrine.|Escalators from the top - Escalators seen against the urban landscape. File:Looking up the escalators at Hie Shrine.jpg|alt=View from below the escalators.|View of escalators through a big torii - Perspective from below. </gallery>
### Torii Gates <gallery mode="packed"> File:Hie Shrine Big Torii with escalators in the background.jpg|alt=A large torii gate with escalators visible in the background.|View of escalators through a big torii - A blend of traditional and modern. File:Hie Shrine big Torii.jpg|alt=A large torii gate marking the entrance to the shrine.|Big Torii - A traditional entry point to sacred space. File:Hie Shrine Torii row.jpg|alt=A row of red torii gates leading to the shrine.|Torii Row - The iconic pathway lined with vibrant red gates. File:Hie Shrine Torii.jpg|alt=A row of red torii gates leading to the shrine.|Torii Row - Another angle showcasing the beauty of the torii gates. </gallery>
### Stairways and Scenic Views <gallery mode="packed"> File:Stairs at Hie Shrine.jpg|alt=Stone stairs leading to the shrine.|Stairs - A classic approach to the shrine for visitors seeking a more traditional journey. File:Hie Shrine Tokyo.jpg|alt=A scenic view of the shrine.|Scenic View - Capturing the serene environment of Hie Shrine in Tokyo. </gallery>
---
## Ōmuroyama Sengen Jinja
### Information and Overview <gallery mode="packed"> File:Omuroyama Asama Jinja information board.jpg|alt=Information board about the shrine.|Information Board - Details about the history and significance of the shrine. File:Omuroyama Sengen Shrine and Crater.jpg|alt=View of the shrine with the crater in the background.|Shrine and Crater - A picturesque view blending nature and spirituality. File:Ōmuroyama Sengen Jinja view.jpg|alt=Panoramic view of the shrine.|View of the Shrine - A wide-angle look at the site’s surroundings. </gallery>
### Buildings and Structures <gallery mode="packed"> File:Ōmuroyama Sengen Jinja Haiden and Sessha.jpg|alt=Main hall and smaller shrine buildings.|Haiden and Sessha - The main hall accompanied by a smaller sacred structure. File:Omuroyama Sengen Shrine secondary building.jpg|alt=Secondary building within the shrine complex.|Secondary Building - Additional structures contributing to the site's spiritual atmosphere. File:Omuroyama Sengen Shrine Senjafuda.jpg|alt=Traditional senjafuda plaques.|Senjafuda - Decorative name plaques left by visitors as offerings. </gallery>
### Waterspouts <gallery mode="packed"> File:Omuroyama Sengen Shrine Waterspout 1.jpg|alt=Traditional waterspout used for purification rituals.|Waterspout - Used for cleansing hands and mouth in purification rituals. File:Omuroyama Sengen Shrine Waterspout 2.jpg|alt=Traditional waterspout.|Waterspout - Another view emphasizing its importance in Shinto practice. </gallery>
### Goshuin <gallery mode="packed"> File:Omuroyama Sengen Shrine Shuin self service stamps.jpg|alt=Self-service stamp station for Goshuin.|Shuin Stamps - A station for visitors to collect commemorative stamps. File:Omuroyama Sengen Shrine Shuin.jpg|alt=Sample Goshuin stamp from the shrine.|Goshuin - Beautiful calligraphy collected as a memento of the visit. </gallery>
### Torii Gates <gallery mode="packed"> File:Omuroyama Sengen Shrine Torii 1.jpg|alt=A torii gate leading to the shrine.|Torii - Traditional gate marking the entrance to sacred grounds. File:View of Omuroyama Torii from the bathroom above.jpg|alt=Unique view of the Torii from above.|Torii View - A unique perspective from higher ground. </gallery>
---
## Ōmuroyama Sengen Jinja From Afar
<gallery mode="packed"> File:Crater of Mount Omuro (Izu Peninsula)-14.jpg|alt=View of Mount Omuro’s crater.|Crater View - Highlighting the natural beauty surrounding the shrine. File:Grassy plains of Mount Omuro- 3290.jpg|alt=Rolling grassy plains near Mount Omuro.|Grassy Plains - Scenic landscapes leading to the shrine. File:View from Mount Ōmuro (Izu Peninsula) taken in 2024-3240.jpg|alt=Panoramic view from Mount Omuro.|Panoramic View - A breathtaking lookout over the surrounding area. </gallery>
---
This format ensures each image is described with relevant information and neatly grouped for ease of viewing. Let me know if you'd like any further refinements!

[[File:Ōmuroyama Sengen Jinja Torii.jpg|thumb|the entrance torii]] Omuroyama Sengen Shrine is a Shrine for [[Iwanaga-hime]]
It has a wikimedia commons Category here [https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/Category%3A%C5%8Cmuroyama_Sengen_Jinja]
== Overview ==
Omuroyama Sengen Shrine is a Shinto shrine located within the crater of Mount Omuro in Ito City, Shizuoka Prefecture, Japan. Established in 1654 by Matsudaira Izumokami, the shrine is dedicated to the deities associated with safe childbirth and matchmaking.
Visitors can reach the summit of Mount Omuro, standing at 580 meters above sea level, via a six-minute chairlift ride. From there, a steep staircase near the lift station leads down into the crater where the shrine is situated. The crater also features an archery range, offering a unique experience for visitors.
The shrine is part of a broader tradition of Sengen shrines in Japan, which are typically dedicated to Konohanasakuya-hime, the deity associated with Mount Fuji and volcanoes. These shrines are often located near volcanic mountains and are linked to prayers for protection against eruptions and natural disasters.
Mount Omuro itself is a prominent feature of the Izu Peninsula, known for its distinctive bowl-shaped silhouette and lush greenery. The mountain offers panoramic views of the surrounding area, including sights of Mount Fuji, the Izu Islands, and Sagami Bay on clear days. An annual event called "Yamayaki," where the entire mountain is set ablaze, takes place on the second Sunday of February, attracting numerous tourists.
For travelers planning a visit, the area around Mount Omuro and Omuroyama Sengen Shrine offers various attractions, including the Izu Shaboten Zoo and scenic walking paths. Accommodations are available nearby, with several hotels and guesthouses catering to different preferences.
Experiencing Omuroyama Sengen Shrine provides insight into Japan's rich cultural heritage and offers a serene environment for reflection amidst natural beauty. == Gallery == {{Commons Category|Ōmuroyama Sengen Jinja}}

[[File:Ōmuroyama Sengen Jinja Torii.jpg|thumb|the entrance torii]] Omuroyama Sengen Shrine is a Shrine for [[Iwanaga-hime]]
It has a wikimedia commons Category here [https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/Category%3A%C5%8Cmuroyama_Sengen_Jinja]
== Overview ==
Omuroyama Sengen Shrine is a Shinto shrine located within the crater of Mount Omuro in Ito City, Shizuoka Prefecture, Japan. Established in 1654 by Matsudaira Izumokami, the shrine is dedicated to the deities associated with safe childbirth and matchmaking.<ref>https://oneclick-onetrip-blog.com/en/shizuoka-prefecture-mt-omuro/?utm_source=chatgpt.com</ref>
Visitors can reach the summit of Mount Omuro, standing at 580 meters above sea level, via a six-minute chairlift ride. From there, a steep staircase near the lift station leads down into the crater where the shrine is situated. The crater also features an archery range, offering a unique experience for visitors.<ref>https://oneclick-onetrip-blog.com/en/shizuoka-prefecture-mt-omuro/?utm_source=chatgpt.com</ref>
The shrine is part of a broader tradition of Sengen shrines in Japan, which are typically dedicated to Konohanasakuya-hime, the deity associated with Mount Fuji and volcanoes. These shrines are often located near volcanic mountains and are linked to prayers for protection against eruptions and natural disasters.<ref>https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sengen_shrine?utm_source=chatgpt.com</ref>
Mount Omuro itself is a prominent feature of the Izu Peninsula, known for its distinctive bowl-shaped silhouette and lush greenery. The mountain offers panoramic views of the surrounding area, including sights of Mount Fuji, the Izu Islands, and Sagami Bay on clear days. An annual event called "Yamayaki," where the entire mountain is set ablaze, takes place on the second Sunday of February, attracting numerous tourists.<ref>https://savorjp.info/scenery/nature/6097/?utm_source=chatgpt.com</ref>
For travelers planning a visit, the area around Mount Omuro and Omuroyama Sengen Shrine offers various attractions, including the Izu Shaboten Zoo and scenic walking paths. Accommodations are available nearby, with several hotels and guesthouses catering to different preferences.<ref>https://www.tripadvisor.ca/HotelsNear-g298123-d10339157-Omuroyama_Sengen_Shrine-Ito_Shizuoka_Prefecture_Tokai_Chubu.html?utm_source=chatgpt.com</ref>
Experiencing Omuroyama Sengen Shrine provides insight into Japan's rich cultural heritage and offers a serene environment for reflection amidst natural beauty. == Gallery == {{Commons Category|Ōmuroyama Sengen Jinja}}

[[File:Ōmuroyama Sengen Jinja Torii.jpg|thumb|the entrance torii]] Omuroyama Sengen Shrine is a Shrine for [[Iwanaga-hime]]
It has a wikimedia commons Category here [https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/Category%3A%C5%8Cmuroyama_Sengen_Jinja]
== Overview ==
Omuroyama Sengen Shrine is a Shinto shrine located within the crater of Mount Omuro in Ito City, Shizuoka Prefecture, Japan. Established in 1654 by Matsudaira Izumokami, the shrine is dedicated to the deities associated with safe childbirth and matchmaking.<ref>https://oneclick-onetrip-blog.com/en/shizuoka-prefecture-mt-omuro/?utm_source=chatgpt.com</ref>
Visitors can reach the summit of Mount Omuro, standing at 580 meters above sea level, via a six-minute chairlift ride. From there, a steep staircase near the lift station leads down into the crater where the shrine is situated. The crater also features an archery range, offering a unique experience for visitors.<ref>https://oneclick-onetrip-blog.com/en/shizuoka-prefecture-mt-omuro/?utm_source=chatgpt.com</ref>
The shrine is part of a broader tradition of Sengen shrines in Japan, which are typically dedicated to Konohanasakuya-hime, the deity associated with Mount Fuji and volcanoes. These shrines are often located near volcanic mountains and are linked to prayers for protection against eruptions and natural disasters.<ref>https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sengen_shrine?utm_source=chatgpt.com</ref>
Mount Omuro itself is a prominent feature of the Izu Peninsula, known for its distinctive bowl-shaped silhouette and lush greenery. The mountain offers panoramic views of the surrounding area, including sights of Mount Fuji, the Izu Islands, and Sagami Bay on clear days. An annual event called "Yamayaki," where the entire mountain is set ablaze, takes place on the second Sunday of February, attracting numerous tourists.<ref>https://savorjp.info/scenery/nature/6097/?utm_source=chatgpt.com</ref>
For travelers planning a visit, the area around Mount Omuro and Omuroyama Sengen Shrine offers various attractions, including the Izu Shaboten Zoo and scenic walking paths. Accommodations are available nearby, with several hotels and guesthouses catering to different preferences.<ref>https://www.tripadvisor.ca/HotelsNear-g298123-d10339157-Omuroyama_Sengen_Shrine-Ito_Shizuoka_Prefecture_Tokai_Chubu.html?utm_source=chatgpt.com</ref>
Experiencing Omuroyama Sengen Shrine provides insight into Japan's rich cultural heritage and offers a serene environment for reflection amidst natural beauty. == Gallery == {{Commons Category|Ōmuroyama Sengen Jinja}}

revision.text:
[[File:Ōmuroyama Sengen Jinja Torii.jpg|thumb|the entrance torii]] Omuroyama Sengen Shrine is a Shrine for [[Iwanaga-hime]]
It has a wikimedia commons Category here [https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/Category%3A%C5%8Cmuroyama_Sengen_Jinja]
== Overview ==
Omuroyama Sengen Shrine is a Shinto shrine located within the crater of Mount Omuro in Ito City, Shizuoka Prefecture, Japan. Established in 1654 by Matsudaira Izumokami, the shrine is dedicated to the deities associated with safe childbirth and matchmaking.<ref>https://oneclick-onetrip-blog.com/en/shizuoka-prefecture-mt-omuro/?utm_source=chatgpt.com</ref>
Visitors can reach the summit of Mount Omuro, standing at 580 meters above sea level, via a six-minute chairlift ride. From there, a steep staircase near the lift station leads down into the crater where the shrine is situated. The crater also features an archery range, offering a unique experience for visitors.<ref>https://oneclick-onetrip-blog.com/en/shizuoka-prefecture-mt-omuro/?utm_source=chatgpt.com</ref>
The shrine is part of a broader tradition of Sengen shrines in Japan, which are typically dedicated to Konohanasakuya-hime, the deity associated with Mount Fuji and volcanoes. These shrines are often located near volcanic mountains and are linked to prayers for protection against eruptions and natural disasters.<ref>https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sengen_shrine?utm_source=chatgpt.com</ref>
Mount Omuro itself is a prominent feature of the Izu Peninsula, known for its distinctive bowl-shaped silhouette and lush greenery. The mountain offers panoramic views of the surrounding area, including sights of Mount Fuji, the Izu Islands, and Sagami Bay on clear days. An annual event called "Yamayaki," where the entire mountain is set ablaze, takes place on the second Sunday of February, attracting numerous tourists.<ref>https://savorjp.info/scenery/nature/6097/?utm_source=chatgpt.com</ref>
For travelers planning a visit, the area around Mount Omuro and Omuroyama Sengen Shrine offers various attractions, including the Izu Shaboten Zoo and scenic walking paths. Accommodations are available nearby, with several hotels and guesthouses catering to different preferences.<ref>https://www.tripadvisor.ca/HotelsNear-g298123-d10339157-Omuroyama_Sengen_Shrine-Ito_Shizuoka_Prefecture_Tokai_Chubu.html?utm_source=chatgpt.com</ref>
Experiencing Omuroyama Sengen Shrine provides insight into Japan's rich cultural heritage and offers a serene environment for reflection amidst natural beauty. == Gallery == {{Commons Category|Ōmuroyama Sengen Jinja}}

==Haiden of Ōmuroyama Sengen Jinja== <gallery mode="packed"> File:Ōmuroyama Sengen Jinja Haiden and Donation box 1.jpg|alt=Haiden and donation box at Ōmuroyama Sengen Jinja.|Haiden and Donation Box - A view of the shrine's main hall with a donation box in the foreground. File:Ōmuroyama Sengen Jinja Haiden and Donation box 2.jpg|alt=Close-up of the Haiden and donation box at Ōmuroyama Sengen Jinja.|Haiden and Donation Box - Closer perspective showcasing the architectural details. File:Ōmuroyama Sengen Jinja Haiden and Donation box 5.jpg|alt=Haiden and donation box at Ōmuroyama Sengen Jinja in a bright setting.|Haiden and Donation Box - Bright lighting emphasizing the vibrant environment. File:Ōmuroyama Sengen Jinja Haiden and Donation box 6.jpg|alt=Haiden and donation box at Ōmuroyama Sengen Jinja with serene surroundings.|Haiden and Donation Box - Another perspective with serene surroundings. revision.text:
[[File:Ōmuroyama Sengen Jinja Torii.jpg|thumb|the entrance torii]] Omuroyama Sengen Shrine is a Shrine for [[Iwanaga-hime]]
It has a wikimedia commons Category here [https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/Category%3A%C5%8Cmuroyama_Sengen_Jinja]
== Overview ==
Omuroyama Sengen Shrine is a Shinto shrine located within the crater of Mount Omuro in Ito City, Shizuoka Prefecture, Japan. Established in 1654 by Matsudaira Izumokami, the shrine is dedicated to the deities associated with safe childbirth and matchmaking.<ref>https://oneclick-onetrip-blog.com/en/shizuoka-prefecture-mt-omuro/?utm_source=chatgpt.com</ref>
Visitors can reach the summit of Mount Omuro, standing at 580 meters above sea level, via a six-minute chairlift ride. From there, a steep staircase near the lift station leads down into the crater where the shrine is situated. The crater also features an archery range, offering a unique experience for visitors.<ref>https://oneclick-onetrip-blog.com/en/shizuoka-prefecture-mt-omuro/?utm_source=chatgpt.com</ref>
The shrine is part of a broader tradition of Sengen shrines in Japan, which are typically dedicated to Konohanasakuya-hime, the deity associated with Mount Fuji and volcanoes. These shrines are often located near volcanic mountains and are linked to prayers for protection against eruptions and natural disasters.<ref>https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sengen_shrine?utm_source=chatgpt.com</ref>
Mount Omuro itself is a prominent feature of the Izu Peninsula, known for its distinctive bowl-shaped silhouette and lush greenery. The mountain offers panoramic views of the surrounding area, including sights of Mount Fuji, the Izu Islands, and Sagami Bay on clear days. An annual event called "Yamayaki," where the entire mountain is set ablaze, takes place on the second Sunday of February, attracting numerous tourists.<ref>https://savorjp.info/scenery/nature/6097/?utm_source=chatgpt.com</ref>
For travelers planning a visit, the area around Mount Omuro and Omuroyama Sengen Shrine offers various attractions, including the Izu Shaboten Zoo and scenic walking paths. Accommodations are available nearby, with several hotels and guesthouses catering to different preferences.<ref>https://www.tripadvisor.ca/HotelsNear-g298123-d10339157-Omuroyama_Sengen_Shrine-Ito_Shizuoka_Prefecture_Tokai_Chubu.html?utm_source=chatgpt.com</ref>
Experiencing Omuroyama Sengen Shrine provides insight into Japan's rich cultural heritage and offers a serene environment for reflection amidst natural beauty. == Gallery == {{Commons Category|Ōmuroyama Sengen Jinja}}
=== Information and Overview === <gallery mode="packed"> File:Omuroyama Asama Jinja information board.jpg|alt=Information board about the shrine.|Information Board - Details about the history and significance of the shrine. File:Omuroyama Sengen Shrine and Crater.jpg|alt=View of the shrine with the crater in the background.|Shrine and Crater - A picturesque view blending nature and spirituality. File:Ōmuroyama Sengen Jinja view.jpg|alt=Panoramic view of the shrine.|View of the Shrine - A wide-angle look at the site’s surroundings. </gallery>
=== Buildings and Structures === <gallery mode="packed"> File:Ōmuroyama Sengen Jinja Haiden and Sessha.jpg|alt=Main hall and smaller shrine buildings.|Haiden and Sessha - The main hall accompanied by a smaller sacred structure. File:Omuroyama Sengen Shrine secondary building.jpg|alt=Secondary building within the shrine complex.|Secondary Building - Additional structures contributing to the site's spiritual atmosphere. File:Omuroyama Sengen Shrine Senjafuda.jpg|alt=Traditional senjafuda plaques.|Senjafuda - Decorative name plaques left by visitors as offerings. </gallery>
=== Waterspouts === <gallery mode="packed"> File:Omuroyama Sengen Shrine Waterspout 1.jpg|alt=Traditional waterspout used for purification rituals.|Waterspout - Used for cleansing hands and mouth in purification rituals. File:Omuroyama Sengen Shrine Waterspout 2.jpg|alt=Traditional waterspout.|Waterspout - Another view emphasizing its importance in Shinto practice. </gallery>
==Haiden of Ōmuroyama Sengen Jinja== <gallery mode="packed"> File:Ōmuroyama Sengen Jinja Haiden and Donation box 1.jpg|alt=Haiden and donation box at Ōmuroyama Sengen Jinja.|Haiden and Donation Box - A view of the shrine's main hall with a donation box in the foreground. File:Ōmuroyama Sengen Jinja Haiden and Donation box 2.jpg|alt=Close-up of the Haiden and donation box at Ōmuroyama Sengen Jinja.|Haiden and Donation Box - Closer perspective showcasing the architectural details. File:Ōmuroyama Sengen Jinja Haiden and Donation box 3.jpg|alt=Haiden and donation box at Ōmuroyama Sengen Jinja with intricate woodwork.|Haiden and Donation Box - Highlighting the intricate woodwork and spiritual ambiance. File:Ōmuroyama Sengen Jinja Haiden and Donation box 4.jpg|alt=Wide view of Haiden and donation box at Ōmuroyama Sengen Jinja.|Haiden and Donation Box - A wider view capturing the surrounding area of the shrine. File:Ōmuroyama Sengen Jinja Haiden and Donation box 5.jpg|alt=Haiden and donation box at Ōmuroyama Sengen Jinja in a bright setting.|Haiden and Donation Box - Bright lighting emphasizing the vibrant environment. File:Ōmuroyama Sengen Jinja Haiden and Donation box 6.jpg|alt=Haiden and donation box at Ōmuroyama Sengen Jinja with serene surroundings.|Haiden and Donation Box - Another perspective with serene surroundings. File:Ōmuroyama Sengen Jinja Haiden stickers.jpg|alt=Stickers and inscriptions on the Haiden at Ōmuroyama Sengen Jinja.|Haiden Stickers - Close-up of decorative stickers and inscriptions adorning the Haiden. </gallery>
=== Goshuin === <gallery mode="packed"> File:Omuroyama Sengen Shrine Shuin self service stamps.jpg|alt=Self-service stamp station for Goshuin.|Shuin Stamps - A station for visitors to collect commemorative stamps. File:Omuroyama Sengen Shrine Shuin.jpg|alt=Sample Goshuin stamp from the shrine.|Goshuin - Beautiful calligraphy collected as a memento of the visit. </gallery>
=== Torii Gates === <gallery mode="packed"> File:Omuroyama Sengen Shrine Torii 1.jpg|alt=A torii gate leading to the shrine.|Torii - Traditional gate marking the entrance to sacred grounds. File:View of Omuroyama Torii from the bathroom above.jpg|alt=Unique view of the Torii from above.|Torii View - A unique perspective from higher ground. </gallery>
---
=== Ōmuroyama Sengen Jinja From Afar === <gallery mode="packed"> File:Crater of Mount Omuro (Izu Peninsula)-14.jpg|alt=View of Mount Omuro’s crater.|Crater View - Highlighting the natural beauty surrounding the shrine. File:Grassy plains of Mount Omuro- 3290.jpg|alt=Rolling grassy plains near Mount Omuro.|Grassy Plains - Scenic landscapes leading to the shrine. File:View from Mount Ōmuro (Izu Peninsula) taken in 2024-3240.jpg|alt=Panoramic view from Mount Omuro.|Panoramic View - A breathtaking lookout over the surrounding area. </gallery>

File:Ōmuroyama Sengen Jinja sessha 2.jpg|alt=Front view of a sessha at Ōmuroyama Sengen Jinja.|Sessha - The front of the sessha, showcasing its traditional architectural design. File:Ōmuroyama Sengen Jinja sessha 3.jpg|alt=Detailed view of a sessha at Ōmuroyama Sengen Jinja.|Sessha - A detailed look at the sessha with intricate woodwork and sacred ambiance. </gallery>

== Overview ==
Omuroyama Sengen Shrine is a Shinto shrine located within the crater of Mount Omuro in Ito City, Shizuoka Prefecture, Japan. Established in 1654 by Matsudaira Izumokami, the shrine is dedicated to the deities associated with safe childbirth and matchmaking.<ref>https://oneclick-onetrip-blog.com/en/shizuoka-prefecture-mt-omuro/?utm_source=chatgpt.com</ref>
Visitors can reach the summit of Mount Omuro, standing at 580 meters above sea level, via a six-minute chairlift ride. From there, a steep staircase near the lift station leads down into the crater where the shrine is situated. The crater also features an archery range, offering a unique experience for visitors.<ref>https://oneclick-onetrip-blog.com/en/shizuoka-prefecture-mt-omuro/?utm_source=chatgpt.com</ref>
The shrine is part of a broader tradition of Sengen shrines in Japan, which are typically dedicated to Konohanasakuya-hime, the deity associated with Mount Fuji and volcanoes. These shrines are often located near volcanic mountains and are linked to prayers for protection against eruptions and natural disasters.<ref>https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sengen_shrine?utm_source=chatgpt.com</ref>
Mount Omuro itself is a prominent feature of the Izu Peninsula, known for its distinctive bowl-shaped silhouette and lush greenery. The mountain offers panoramic views of the surrounding area, including sights of Mount Fuji, the Izu Islands, and Sagami Bay on clear days. An annual event called "Yamayaki," where the entire mountain is set ablaze, takes place on the second Sunday of February, attracting numerous tourists.<ref>https://savorjp.info/scenery/nature/6097/?utm_source=chatgpt.com</ref>
For travelers planning a visit, the area around Mount Omuro and Omuroyama Sengen Shrine offers various attractions, including the Izu Shaboten Zoo and scenic walking paths. Accommodations are available nearby, with several hotels and guesthouses catering to different preferences.<ref>https://www.tripadvisor.ca/HotelsNear-g298123-d10339157-Omuroyama_Sengen_Shrine-Ito_Shizuoka_Prefecture_Tokai_Chubu.html?utm_source=chatgpt.com</ref>
Experiencing Omuroyama Sengen Shrine provides insight into Japan's rich cultural heritage and offers a serene environment for reflection amidst natural beauty. == Gallery == {{Commons Category|Ōmuroyama Sengen Jinja}}

== Overview ==
Omuroyama Sengen Shrine is a Shinto shrine located within the crater of Mount Omuro in Ito City, Shizuoka Prefecture, Japan. Established in 1654 by Matsudaira Izumokami, the shrine is dedicated to the deities associated with safe childbirth and matchmaking.<ref>https://oneclick-onetrip-blog.com/en/shizuoka-prefecture-mt-omuro/?utm_source=chatgpt.com</ref>
Visitors can reach the summit of Mount Omuro, standing at 580 meters above sea level, via a six-minute chairlift ride. From there, a steep staircase near the lift station leads down into the crater where the shrine is situated. The crater also features an archery range, offering a unique experience for visitors.<ref>https://oneclick-onetrip-blog.com/en/shizuoka-prefecture-mt-omuro/?utm_source=chatgpt.com</ref>
The shrine is part of a broader tradition of Sengen shrines in Japan, which are typically dedicated to Konohanasakuya-hime, the deity associated with Mount Fuji and volcanoes. These shrines are often located near volcanic mountains and are linked to prayers for protection against eruptions and natural disasters.<ref>https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sengen_shrine?utm_source=chatgpt.com</ref>
Mount Omuro itself is a prominent feature of the Izu Peninsula, known for its distinctive bowl-shaped silhouette and lush greenery. The mountain offers panoramic views of the surrounding area, including sights of Mount Fuji, the Izu Islands, and Sagami Bay on clear days. An annual event called "Yamayaki," where the entire mountain is set ablaze, takes place on the second Sunday of February, attracting numerous tourists.<ref>https://savorjp.info/scenery/nature/6097/?utm_source=chatgpt.com</ref>
For travelers planning a visit, the area around Mount Omuro and Omuroyama Sengen Shrine offers various attractions, including the Izu Shaboten Zoo and scenic walking paths. Accommodations are available nearby, with several hotels and guesthouses catering to different preferences.<ref>https://www.tripadvisor.ca/HotelsNear-g298123-d10339157-Omuroyama_Sengen_Shrine-Ito_Shizuoka_Prefecture_Tokai_Chubu.html?utm_source=chatgpt.com</ref>
Experiencing Omuroyama Sengen Shrine provides insight into Japan's rich cultural heritage and offers a serene environment for reflection amidst natural beauty. == Mythology == Ōmuroyama Sengen Shrine is dedicated to Iwanaga hime, where she is worshipped through a [[Kannabi]] that represents her residing directly within the mountain as her [[shintai]].<ref name=":2">{{Cite web|title=パワースポット|url=https://omuroyama.com/event/powerspot/|access-date=2024-05-17|website=大室山登山リフトオフィシャルサイト}}</ref>
Omuroyama itself was once believed to be divine. Now houses Sengen Shrine on its mid slope near the crater.<ref name=":2" />
The shrine houses the deity Iwanaga hime no mikoto, known for her connection, to safe childbirth and matchmaking. She is the sister of Konohanasakuya hime the goddess revered at the peak of Mount Fuji.<ref name=":2" />
Located on the summit along a mountain path are five life sized Wisdom Buddhas (Gochi Nyorai). 300 years ago a nine year old girl, daughter of Asakura Seibee, an official from Iwamura in Sagami Province (now Ashigarakami District, Kanagawa Prefecture) had a safe delivery thanks to prayers offered at Omuroyama Sengen Shrine. In gratitude for this blessing these Buddhas were enshrined.<ref name=":2" />
In times local fishermen sought protection and safety from sea disasters, at Omuroyama facing Oshima Island. This led to the dedication of eight Jizo statues known as Yatsugatake Jizo at the summit of Omuroyama.<ref name=":2" />
At Omuroyama stands the haiku monument honoring Takaha Shugyo, who admired and cherished the Izu region alongside his master Akimoto Fujio.<ref name=":2" />
The haiku says, "Izu is where the sun gently falls, blooming with mandarin flowers " beautifully capturing the beauty of Izus landscape in the summer.<ref name=":2" />

== Overview ==
Omuroyama Sengen Shrine is a Shinto shrine located within the crater of Mount Omuro in Ito City, Shizuoka Prefecture, Japan. Established in 1654 by Matsudaira Izumokami, the shrine is dedicated to the deities associated with safe childbirth and matchmaking.<ref>https://oneclick-onetrip-blog.com/en/shizuoka-prefecture-mt-omuro/?utm_source=chatgpt.com</ref>
Visitors can reach the summit of Mount Omuro, standing at 580 meters above sea level, via a six-minute chairlift ride. From there, a steep staircase near the lift station leads down into the crater where the shrine is situated. The crater also features an archery range, offering a unique experience for visitors.<ref>https://oneclick-onetrip-blog.com/en/shizuoka-prefecture-mt-omuro/?utm_source=chatgpt.com</ref>
The shrine is part of a broader tradition of Sengen shrines in Japan, which are typically dedicated to Konohanasakuya-hime, the deity associated with Mount Fuji and volcanoes. These shrines are often located near volcanic mountains and are linked to prayers for protection against eruptions and natural disasters.<ref>https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sengen_shrine?utm_source=chatgpt.com</ref>
Mount Omuro itself is a prominent feature of the Izu Peninsula, known for its distinctive bowl-shaped silhouette and lush greenery. The mountain offers panoramic views of the surrounding area, including sights of Mount Fuji, the Izu Islands, and Sagami Bay on clear days. An annual event called "Yamayaki," where the entire mountain is set ablaze, takes place on the second Sunday of February, attracting numerous tourists.<ref>https://savorjp.info/scenery/nature/6097/?utm_source=chatgpt.com</ref>
For travelers planning a visit, the area around Mount Omuro and Omuroyama Sengen Shrine offers various attractions, including the Izu Shaboten Zoo and scenic walking paths. Accommodations are available nearby, with several hotels and guesthouses catering to different preferences.<ref>https://www.tripadvisor.ca/HotelsNear-g298123-d10339157-Omuroyama_Sengen_Shrine-Ito_Shizuoka_Prefecture_Tokai_Chubu.html?utm_source=chatgpt.com</ref>
Experiencing Omuroyama Sengen Shrine provides insight into Japan's rich cultural heritage and offers a serene environment for reflection amidst natural beauty. == Mythology == Ōmuroyama Sengen Shrine is dedicated to Iwanaga hime, where she is worshipped through a [[Kannabi]] that represents her residing directly within the mountain as her [[shintai]].<ref name=":2">{{Cite web|title=パワースポット|url=https://omuroyama.com/event/powerspot/|access-date=2024-05-17|website=大室山登山リフトオフィシャルサイト}}</ref>
Omuroyama itself was once believed to be divine. Now houses Sengen Shrine on its mid slope near the crater.<ref name=":2" />
The shrine houses the deity Iwanaga hime no mikoto, known for her connection, to safe childbirth and matchmaking. She is the sister of Konohanasakuya hime the goddess revered at the peak of Mount Fuji.<ref name=":2" />
Located on the summit along a mountain path are five life sized Wisdom Buddhas (Gochi Nyorai). 300 years ago a nine year old girl, daughter of Asakura Seibee, an official from Iwamura in Sagami Province (now Ashigarakami District, Kanagawa Prefecture) had a safe delivery thanks to prayers offered at Omuroyama Sengen Shrine. In gratitude for this blessing these Buddhas were enshrined.<ref name=":2" />
In times local fishermen sought protection and safety from sea disasters, at Omuroyama facing Oshima Island. This led to the dedication of eight Jizo statues known as Yatsugatake Jizo at the summit of Omuroyama.<ref name=":2" />
At Omuroyama stands the haiku monument honoring Takaha Shugyo, who admired and cherished the Izu region alongside his master Akimoto Fujio.<ref name=":2" />
The haiku says, "Izu is where the sun gently falls, blooming with mandarin flowers " beautifully capturing the beauty of Izus landscape in the summer.<ref name=":2" />

It has a wikimedia commons Category here [https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/Category%3A%C5%8Cmuroyama_Sengen_Jinja]
== Overview ==
Omuroyama Sengen Shrine is a Shinto shrine located within the crater of Mount Omuro in Ito City, Shizuoka Prefecture, Japan. Established in 1654 by Matsudaira Izumokami, the shrine is dedicated to the deities associated with safe childbirth and matchmaking.<ref>https://oneclick-onetrip-blog.com/en/shizuoka-prefecture-mt-omuro/?utm_source=chatgpt.com</ref>
Visitors can reach the summit of Mount Omuro, standing at 580 meters above sea level, via a six-minute chairlift ride. From there, a steep staircase near the lift station leads down into the crater where the shrine is situated. The crater also features an archery range, offering a unique experience for visitors.<ref>https://oneclick-onetrip-blog.com/en/shizuoka-prefecture-mt-omuro/?utm_source=chatgpt.com</ref>
The shrine is part of a broader tradition of Sengen shrines in Japan, which are typically dedicated to Konohanasakuya-hime, the deity associated with Mount Fuji and volcanoes. These shrines are often located near volcanic mountains and are linked to prayers for protection against eruptions and natural disasters.<ref>https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sengen_shrine?utm_source=chatgpt.com</ref>
Mount Omuro itself is a prominent feature of the Izu Peninsula, known for its distinctive bowl-shaped silhouette and lush greenery. The mountain offers panoramic views of the surrounding area, including sights of Mount Fuji, the Izu Islands, and Sagami Bay on clear days. An annual event called "Yamayaki," where the entire mountain is set ablaze, takes place on the second Sunday of February, attracting numerous tourists.<ref>https://savorjp.info/scenery/nature/6097/?utm_source=chatgpt.com</ref>
For travelers planning a visit, the area around Mount Omuro and Omuroyama Sengen Shrine offers various attractions, including the Izu Shaboten Zoo and scenic walking paths. Accommodations are available nearby, with several hotels and guesthouses catering to different preferences.<ref>https://www.tripadvisor.ca/HotelsNear-g298123-d10339157-Omuroyama_Sengen_Shrine-Ito_Shizuoka_Prefecture_Tokai_Chubu.html?utm_source=chatgpt.com</ref>
Experiencing Omuroyama Sengen Shrine provides insight into Japan's rich cultural heritage and offers a serene environment for reflection amidst natural beauty. == Mythology == Ōmuroyama Sengen Shrine is dedicated to Iwanaga hime, where she is worshipped through a [[Kannabi]] that represents her residing directly within the mountain as her [[shintai]].<ref name=":2">{{Cite web|title=パワースポット|url=https://omuroyama.com/event/powerspot/|access-date=2024-05-17|website=大室山登山リフトオフィシャルサイト}}</ref>
Omuroyama itself was once believed to be divine. Now houses Sengen Shrine on its mid slope near the crater.<ref name=":2" />
The shrine houses the deity Iwanaga hime no mikoto, known for her connection, to safe childbirth and matchmaking. She is the sister of Konohanasakuya hime the goddess revered at the peak of Mount Fuji.<ref name=":2" />
Located on the summit along a mountain path are five life sized Wisdom Buddhas (Gochi Nyorai). 300 years ago a nine year old girl, daughter of Asakura Seibee, an official from Iwamura in Sagami Province (now Ashigarakami District, Kanagawa Prefecture) had a safe delivery thanks to prayers offered at Omuroyama Sengen Shrine. In gratitude for this blessing these Buddhas were enshrined.<ref name=":2" />
In times local fishermen sought protection and safety from sea disasters, at Omuroyama facing Oshima Island. This led to the dedication of eight Jizo statues known as Yatsugatake Jizo at the summit of Omuroyama.<ref name=":2" />
At Omuroyama stands the haiku monument honoring Takaha Shugyo, who admired and cherished the Izu region alongside his master Akimoto Fujio.<ref name=":2" />
The haiku says, "Izu is where the sun gently falls, blooming with mandarin flowers " beautifully capturing the beauty of Izus landscape in the summer.<ref name=":2" />

== 神社・石仏 ==
* '''大室山浅間神社'''<ref name="asama">出典 : [http://itospa.com/sanpo/wp-content/uploads/2010/10/omuroyama101027-19.jpg 現地解説版]、2016年1月閲覧</ref> - 火口内の東側中腹にある神社。磐長姫命（いわながひめのみこと）を唯一の祭神とする。安産と縁結びの神として信仰されている。浅間大神（あさまのおおかみ）と木花之佐久夜毘売（このはなさくやびめ）を主祭神とする浅間神社にあって、磐長姫命を祀るのは稀である<ref name="Arakawa2008">{{Cite journal|和書|author=荒川理恵|title=石長比売の霊力|journal=学習院大学上代文学研究|volume=33|date=2008-03-31|pages=1-26|issn=0916-2453}}</ref>。姉の磐長姫命と妹の木花之佐久夜毘売は、瓊々杵命（ににぎのみこと）に同時に嫁いだが、醜い姉神は送り返された<ref>古事記では妹神の木花之佐久夜毘売が燃える産屋で火の神を出産する。しかし、現地解説板（2018年閲覧）では送り返された姉神の磐長姫命が大室山の燃える茅の産屋で火の神を出産したとする。</ref>。大室山と同様、妹神を祀る富士山を望む独立峰である烏帽子山（静岡県賀茂郡松崎町）の雲見浅間神社も姉神を祀る。
* '''五智如来地蔵尊'''<ref name="gochi">出典 : [http://obtweb.typepad.jp/.a/6a0120a877a494970b0192ac3acebb970d-800wi 現地解説板]、2016年1月閲覧</ref> - 火口縁西側にある江戸時代に建てられた5体の仏像。縁結びと安産を願って参拝客が訪れる。1663年（寛文3年）、相模国岩村（現・神奈川県真鶴町）の網元朝倉清兵衛<ref name="odawaracity">朝倉清兵衛について、現地解説板(2018年閲覧)は網元としている。参考として、[https://www.city.odawara.kanagawa.jp/field/lifelong/property/cultural/rekishishiryou/rekishichoukouzan.html 小田原市歴史資料](2011年1月)に岩村の同名の石工が寛文9年(1669年)に紹太寺建立に従事したとの記載があることをここに挙げておく。</ref>が、大室山浅間神社に9歳の娘の安産を祈願し、無事出産のお礼として建てた五智如来像<ref>現地解説板(2018年閲覧)では表題に「五智如来地蔵尊」、解説文に「五智如来蔵を作り」と書かれている。しかし、石仏の形は地蔵ではなく如来であること、また、如来蔵は石を彫って作る対象ではないことから、「五智如来像」とした。</ref>とされる。真鶴石で作り、富戸港から運び上げたと伝えられている。
* '''八ケ岳地蔵尊'''<ref name="yatsu">出典 : [http://canned-seachicken.up.n.seesaa.net/canned-seachicken/image/E585ABE383B6E5B2B3E59CB0E894B5E5B08AE381AEE794B1E69DA5.jpg 現地解説板]、2016年1月閲覧</ref> - 火口縁南側にある8体の地蔵。大室山を目印としていた漁師たちが海上安全と豊漁を祈願して建てた。新旧あり、手前は1984年（昭和59年）に池区民の寄付によりつくられたもの。 <gallery> ファイル:Itoh_20181124_115026.jpg|link=https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E3%83%95%E3%82%A1%E3%82%A4%E3%83%AB:Itoh_20181124_115026.jpg|五智如来像 ファイル:Itoh_20181124_113718.jpg|link=https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E3%83%95%E3%82%A1%E3%82%A4%E3%83%AB:Itoh_20181124_113718.jpg|八ケ岳地蔵尊 ファイル:Ōmuroyama_Sengen_Jinja_Haiden_and_Sessha.jpg|link=https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E3%83%95%E3%82%A1%E3%82%A4%E3%83%AB:%C5%8Cmuroyama_Sengen_Jinja_Haiden_and_Sessha.jpg|大室山浅間神社 </gallery>

Visitors can reach the summit of Mount Omuro, standing at 580 meters above sea level, via a six-minute chairlift ride. The crater also features an archery range, offering a unique experience for visitors.<ref>https://oneclick-onetrip-blog.com/en/shizuoka-prefecture-mt-omuro/?utm_source=chatgpt.com</ref>
The shrine is part of a broader tradition of Sengen shrines in Japan, which are typically dedicated to Konohanasakuya-hime, the deity associated with Mount Fuji and volcanoes. These shrines are often located near volcanic mountains and are linked to prayers for protection against eruptions and natural disasters.<ref>https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sengen_shrine?utm_source=chatgpt.com</ref>
Mount Omuro itself is a prominent feature of the Izu Peninsula, known for its distinctive bowl-shaped silhouette and lush greenery. The mountain offers panoramic views of the surrounding area, including sights of Mount Fuji, the Izu Islands, and Sagami Bay on clear days. An annual event called "Yamayaki," where the entire mountain is set ablaze, takes place on the second Sunday of February, attracting numerous tourists.<ref>https://savorjp.info/scenery/nature/6097/?utm_source=chatgpt.com</ref>
For travelers planning a visit, the area around Mount Omuro and Omuroyama Sengen Shrine offers various attractions, including the Izu Shaboten Zoo and scenic walking paths. Accommodations are available nearby, with several hotels and guesthouses catering to different preferences.<ref>https://www.tripadvisor.ca/HotelsNear-g298123-d10339157-Omuroyama_Sengen_Shrine-Ito_Shizuoka_Prefecture_Tokai_Chubu.html?utm_source=chatgpt.com</ref>
Experiencing Omuroyama Sengen Shrine provides insight into Japan's rich cultural heritage and offers a serene environment for reflection amidst natural beauty. == Mythology == Ōmuroyama Sengen Shrine is dedicated to Iwanaga hime, where she is worshipped through a [[Kannabi]] that represents her residing directly within the mountain as her [[shintai]].<ref name=":2">{{Cite web|title=パワースポット|url=https://omuroyama.com/event/powerspot/|access-date=2024-05-17|website=大室山登山リフトオフィシャルサイト}}</ref>
Omuroyama itself was once believed to be divine. Now houses Sengen Shrine on its mid slope near the crater.<ref name=":2" />
The shrine houses the deity Iwanaga hime no mikoto, known for her connection, to safe childbirth and matchmaking. She is the sister of Konohanasakuya hime the goddess revered at the peak of Mount Fuji.<ref name=":2" />
Located on the summit along a mountain path are five life sized Wisdom Buddhas (Gochi Nyorai). 300 years ago a nine year old girl, daughter of Asakura Seibee, an official from Iwamura in Sagami Province (now Ashigarakami District, Kanagawa Prefecture) had a safe delivery thanks to prayers offered at Omuroyama Sengen Shrine. In gratitude for this blessing these Buddhas were enshrined.<ref name=":2" />
In times local fishermen sought protection and safety from sea disasters, at Omuroyama facing Oshima Island. This led to the dedication of eight Jizo statues known as Yatsugatake Jizo at the summit of Omuroyama.<ref name=":2" />
At Omuroyama stands the haiku monument honoring Takaha Shugyo, who admired and cherished the Izu region alongside his master Akimoto Fujio.<ref name=":2" />
The haiku says, "Izu is where the sun gently falls, blooming with mandarin flowers " beautifully capturing the beauty of Izus landscape in the summer.<ref name=":2" />
== Shrines & Stone Buddhas ==
* '''Ōmuroyama Sengen Shrine''' (大室山浅間神社)<ref name="asama">Source: [On-site explanation board](http://itospa.com/sanpo/wp-content/uploads/2010/10/omuroyama101027-19.jpg), accessed January 2016</ref> - A shrine located on the eastern mid-slope within the crater. It enshrines {{ill|Iwanaga-hime no Mikoto|ja|磐長姫命}} as its sole deity. She is worshipped as a goddess of safe childbirth and matchmaking. While most {{ill|Sengen Shrines|ja|浅間神社}} primarily enshrine {{ill|Asama no Ōkami|ja|浅間大神}} and {{ill|Konohanasakuya-hime|ja|木花之佐久夜毘売}}, it is rare to find one that venerates Iwanaga-hime no Mikoto<ref name="Arakawa2008">{{Cite journal|Japanese|author=Rie Arakawa|title=The Spiritual Power of Ishinaga-hime|journal=Gakushuin University Studies in Ancient Literature|volume=33|date=March 31, 2008|pages=1-26|issn=0916-2453}}</ref>. Iwanaga-hime, the elder sister, and Konohanasakuya-hime, the younger sister, were both married to {{ill|Ninigi no Mikoto|ja|瓊々杵命}}, but the elder sister, considered unattractive, was sent back<ref>In the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}, Konohanasakuya-hime gives birth to the fire god in a burning birthing hut. However, according to the on-site explanation board (viewed in 2018), it is said that Iwanaga-hime, who was sent back, gave birth to the fire god in a burning thatched hut on Ōmuroyama.</ref>. Like Ōmuroyama, {{ill|Kumomi Sengen Shrine|ja|雲見浅間神社}} on Mount Eboshi (in {{ill|Matsuzaki, Shizuoka|ja|松崎町}}, Kamo District, Shizuoka Prefecture), an isolated peak with views of Mount Fuji, also enshrines the elder sister.
* '''Gochi Nyorai Jizō-son''' (五智如来地蔵尊)<ref name="gochi">Source: [On-site explanation board](http://obtweb.typepad.jp/.a/6a0120a877a494970b0192ac3acebb970d-800wi), accessed January 2016</ref> - Five statues of Buddha located on the western edge of the crater, built during the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}. Visitors come to pray for good relationships and safe childbirth. In 1663 (Kanbun 3), {{ill|Asakura Seibei|ja|朝倉清兵衛}}<ref name="odawaracity">Regarding Asakura Seibei, the on-site explanation board (viewed in 2018) describes him as a fishing boss. As a reference, [Odawara City Historical Materials](https://www.city.odawara.kanagawa.jp/field/lifelong/property/cultural/rekishishiryou/rekishichoukouzan.html) (January 2011) mentions a stonemason of the same name from {{ill|Iwamura, Kanagawa|ja|岩村}} who worked on the construction of Shōtaiji Temple in Kanbun 9 (1669).</ref>, a fishing boss from Iwamura in {{ill|Sagami Province|ja|相模国}} (now {{ill|Manazuru, Kanagawa|ja|真鶴町}}, Kanagawa Prefecture), prayed at Ōmuroyama Sengen Shrine for the safe childbirth of his 9-year-old daughter. In gratitude for her safe delivery, he erected these statues of the {{ill|Five Wisdom Buddhas|ja|五智如来}}<ref>On the on-site explanation board (viewed in 2018), the title reads "Gochi Nyorai Jizō-son," and the explanatory text mentions the creation of "Gochi Nyorai-zō." However, since the stone statues resemble Buddhas rather than Jizō, and because Nyorai-zō refers to carved stone figures, "Five Wisdom Buddha Statues" is used here.</ref>. They are said to be made of {{ill|Manazuru stone|ja|真鶴石}} and were transported from {{ill|Futo Port|ja|富戸港}}.
* '''Yatsugatake Jizō-son''' (八ケ岳地蔵尊)<ref name="yatsu">Source: [On-site explanation board](http://canned-seachicken.up.n.seesaa.net/canned-seachicken/image/E585ABE383B6E5B2B3E59CB0E894B5E5B08AE381AEE794B1E69DA5.jpg), accessed January 2016</ref> - Eight {{ill|Jizō|ja|地蔵}} statues located on the southern edge of the crater. They were erected by fishermen who used Ōmuroyama as a landmark, praying for maritime safety and good catches. There are both old and new statues, with the front ones built in 1984 (Shōwa 59) through donations from local residents of {{ill|Ike District|ja|池区}}.
<gallery> File:Itoh_20181124_115026.jpg|link=https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E3%83%95%E3%82%A1%E3%82%A4%E3%83%AB:Itoh_20181124_115026.jpg|Five Wisdom Buddha Statues File:Itoh_20181124_113718.jpg|link=https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E3%83%95%E3%82%A1%E3%82%A4%E3%83%AB:Itoh_20181124_113718.jpg|Yatsugatake Jizō-son File:Ōmuroyama_Sengen_Jinja_Haiden_and_Sessha.jpg|link=https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E3%83%95%E3%82%A1%E3%82%A4%E3%83%AB:%C5%8Cmuroyama_Sengen_Jinja_Haiden_and_Sessha.jpg|Ōmuroyama Sengen Shrine </gallery>

== Overview ==
Omuroyama Sengen Shrine is a Shinto shrine located within the crater of Mount Omuro in Ito City, Shizuoka Prefecture, Japan. Established in 1654 by Matsudaira Izumokami, the shrine is dedicated to the deities associated with safe childbirth and matchmaking.<ref>https://oneclick-onetrip-blog.com/en/shizuoka-prefecture-mt-omuro/?utm_source=chatgpt.com</ref>
Visitors can reach the summit of Mount Omuro, standing at 580 meters above sea level, via a six-minute chairlift ride. From there, a steep staircase near the lift station leads down into the crater where the shrine is situated. The crater also features an archery range, offering a unique experience for visitors.<ref>https://oneclick-onetrip-blog.com/en/shizuoka-prefecture-mt-omuro/?utm_source=chatgpt.com</ref>
The shrine is part of a broader tradition of Sengen shrines in Japan, which are typically dedicated to Konohanasakuya-hime, the deity associated with Mount Fuji and volcanoes. These shrines are often located near volcanic mountains and are linked to prayers for protection against eruptions and natural disasters.<ref>https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sengen_shrine?utm_source=chatgpt.com</ref>
Mount Omuro itself is a prominent feature of the Izu Peninsula, known for its distinctive bowl-shaped silhouette and lush greenery. The mountain offers panoramic views of the surrounding area, including sights of Mount Fuji, the Izu Islands, and Sagami Bay on clear days. An annual event called "Yamayaki," where the entire mountain is set ablaze, takes place on the second Sunday of February, attracting numerous tourists.<ref>https://savorjp.info/scenery/nature/6097/?utm_source=chatgpt.com</ref>
For travelers planning a visit, the area around Mount Omuro and Omuroyama Sengen Shrine offers various attractions, including the Izu Shaboten Zoo and scenic walking paths. Accommodations are available nearby, with several hotels and guesthouses catering to different preferences.<ref>https://www.tripadvisor.ca/HotelsNear-g298123-d10339157-Omuroyama_Sengen_Shrine-Ito_Shizuoka_Prefecture_Tokai_Chubu.html?utm_source=chatgpt.com</ref>
Experiencing Omuroyama Sengen Shrine provides insight into Japan's rich cultural heritage and offers a serene environment for reflection amidst natural beauty. [[ja:大室山浅間神社]]
== Mythology == Ōmuroyama Sengen Shrine is dedicated to Iwanaga hime, where she is worshipped through a [[Kannabi]] that represents her residing directly within the mountain as her [[shintai]].<ref name=":2">{{Cite web|title=パワースポット|url=https://omuroyama.com/event/powerspot/|access-date=2024-05-17|website=大室山登山リフトオフィシャルサイト}}</ref>
Omuroyama itself was once believed to be divine. Now houses Sengen Shrine on its mid slope near the crater.<ref name=":2" />
The shrine houses the deity Iwanaga hime no mikoto, known for her connection, to safe childbirth and matchmaking. She is the sister of Konohanasakuya hime the goddess revered at the peak of Mount Fuji.<ref name=":2" />
Located on the summit along a mountain path are five life sized Wisdom Buddhas (Gochi Nyorai). 300 years ago a nine year old girl, daughter of Asakura Seibee, an official from Iwamura in Sagami Province (now Ashigarakami District, Kanagawa Prefecture) had a safe delivery thanks to prayers offered at Omuroyama Sengen Shrine. In gratitude for this blessing these Buddhas were enshrined.<ref name=":2" />
In times local fishermen sought protection and safety from sea disasters, at Omuroyama facing Oshima Island. This led to the dedication of eight Jizo statues known as Yatsugatake Jizo at the summit of Omuroyama.<ref name=":2" />
At Omuroyama stands the haiku monument honoring Takaha Shugyo, who admired and cherished the Izu region alongside his master Akimoto Fujio.<ref name=":2" />
The haiku says, "Izu is where the sun gently falls, blooming with mandarin flowers " beautifully capturing the beauty of Izus landscape in the summer.<ref name=":2" />

revision.text:
{{distinguish|{{ill|Omuro Sengen Shrine|ja|大室山浅間神社}}|{{ill|Omuro Sengen Shrine (Ōasomi, Fujiyoshida)|ja|小室浅間神社_(富士吉田市大明見)}}|{{ill|Fuji Omuro Asama Shrine|ja|冨士御室浅間神社}}}} [[File:Ōmuroyama Sengen Jinja Torii.jpg|thumb|the entrance torii]] Omuroyama Sengen Shrine (大室山浅間神社) is a Shrine for [[Iwanaga-hime]]. It is present on [[Mount Ōmuro (Shizuoka)|Mount Ōmuro]]
It has a wikimedia commons Category here [https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/Category%3A%C5%8Cmuroyama_Sengen_Jinja]
== Overview ==
Omuroyama Sengen Shrine is a Shinto shrine located within the crater of Mount Omuro in Ito City, Shizuoka Prefecture, Japan. Established in 1654 by Matsudaira Izumokami, the shrine is dedicated to the deities associated with safe childbirth and matchmaking.<ref>https://oneclick-onetrip-blog.com/en/shizuoka-prefecture-mt-omuro/?utm_source=chatgpt.com</ref>
Visitors can reach the summit of Mount Omuro, standing at 580 meters above sea level, via a six-minute chairlift ride. From there, a steep staircase near the lift station leads down into the crater where the shrine is situated. The crater also features an archery range, offering a unique experience for visitors.<ref>https://oneclick-onetrip-blog.com/en/shizuoka-prefecture-mt-omuro/?utm_source=chatgpt.com</ref>
The shrine is part of a broader tradition of Sengen shrines in Japan, which are typically dedicated to Konohanasakuya-hime, the deity associated with Mount Fuji and volcanoes. These shrines are often located near volcanic mountains and are linked to prayers for protection against eruptions and natural disasters.<ref>https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sengen_shrine?utm_source=chatgpt.com</ref>
Mount Omuro itself is a prominent feature of the Izu Peninsula, known for its distinctive bowl-shaped silhouette and lush greenery. The mountain offers panoramic views of the surrounding area, including sights of Mount Fuji, the Izu Islands, and Sagami Bay on clear days. An annual event called "Yamayaki," where the entire mountain is set ablaze, takes place on the second Sunday of February, attracting numerous tourists.<ref>https://savorjp.info/scenery/nature/6097/?utm_source=chatgpt.com</ref>
For travelers planning a visit, the area around Mount Omuro and Omuroyama Sengen Shrine offers various attractions, including the Izu Shaboten Zoo and scenic walking paths. Accommodations are available nearby, with several hotels and guesthouses catering to different preferences.<ref>https://www.tripadvisor.ca/HotelsNear-g298123-d10339157-Omuroyama_Sengen_Shrine-Ito_Shizuoka_Prefecture_Tokai_Chubu.html?utm_source=chatgpt.com</ref>
Experiencing Omuroyama Sengen Shrine provides insight into Japan's rich cultural heritage and offers a serene environment for reflection amidst natural beauty. [[ja:大室山浅間神社]]
== Mythology == Ōmuroyama Sengen Shrine is dedicated to Iwanaga hime, where she is worshipped through a [[Kannabi]] that represents her residing directly within the mountain as her [[shintai]].<ref name=":2">{{Cite web|title=パワースポット|url=https://omuroyama.com/event/powerspot/|access-date=2024-05-17|website=大室山登山リフトオフィシャルサイト}}</ref>
Omuroyama itself was once believed to be divine. Now houses Sengen Shrine on its mid slope near the crater.<ref name=":2" />
The shrine houses the deity Iwanaga hime no mikoto, known for her connection, to safe childbirth and matchmaking. She is the sister of Konohanasakuya hime the goddess revered at the peak of Mount Fuji.<ref name=":2" />
Located on the summit along a mountain path are five life sized Wisdom Buddhas (Gochi Nyorai). 300 years ago a nine year old girl, daughter of Asakura Seibee, an official from Iwamura in Sagami Province (now Ashigarakami District, Kanagawa Prefecture) had a safe delivery thanks to prayers offered at Omuroyama Sengen Shrine. In gratitude for this blessing these Buddhas were enshrined.<ref name=":2" />
In times local fishermen sought protection and safety from sea disasters, at Omuroyama facing Oshima Island. This led to the dedication of eight Jizo statues known as Yatsugatake Jizo at the summit of Omuroyama.<ref name=":2" />
At Omuroyama stands the haiku monument honoring Takaha Shugyo, who admired and cherished the Izu region alongside his master Akimoto Fujio.<ref name=":2" />
The haiku says, "Izu is where the sun gently falls, blooming with mandarin flowers " beautifully capturing the beauty of Izus landscape in the summer.<ref name=":2" />
== Shrines & Stone Buddhas ==
* '''Ōmuroyama Sengen Shrine''' (大室山浅間神社)<ref name="asama">Source: [On-site explanation board](http://itospa.com/sanpo/wp-content/uploads/2010/10/omuroyama101027-19.jpg), accessed January 2016</ref> - A shrine located on the eastern mid-slope within the crater. It enshrines {{ill|Iwanaga-hime no Mikoto|ja|磐長姫命}} as its sole deity. She is worshipped as a goddess of safe childbirth and matchmaking. While most {{ill|Sengen Shrines|ja|浅間神社}} primarily enshrine {{ill|Asama no Ōkami|ja|浅間大神}} and {{ill|Konohanasakuya-hime|ja|木花之佐久夜毘売}}, it is rare to find one that venerates Iwanaga-hime no Mikoto<ref name="Arakawa2008">{{Cite journal|Japanese|author=Rie Arakawa|title=The Spiritual Power of Ishinaga-hime|journal=Gakushuin University Studies in Ancient Literature|volume=33|date=March 31, 2008|pages=1-26|issn=0916-2453}}</ref>. Iwanaga-hime, the elder sister, and Konohanasakuya-hime, the younger sister, were both married to {{ill|Ninigi no Mikoto|ja|瓊々杵命}}, but the elder sister, considered unattractive, was sent back<ref>In the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}, Konohanasakuya-hime gives birth to the fire god in a burning birthing hut. However, according to the on-site explanation board (viewed in 2018), it is said that Iwanaga-hime, who was sent back, gave birth to the fire god in a burning thatched hut on Ōmuroyama.</ref>. Like Ōmuroyama, {{ill|Kumomi Sengen Shrine|ja|雲見浅間神社}} on Mount Eboshi (in {{ill|Matsuzaki, Shizuoka|ja|松崎町}}, Kamo District, Shizuoka Prefecture), an isolated peak with views of Mount Fuji, also enshrines the elder sister.
* '''Gochi Nyorai Jizō-son''' (五智如来地蔵尊)<ref name="gochi">Source: [On-site explanation board](http://obtweb.typepad.jp/.a/6a0120a877a494970b0192ac3acebb970d-800wi), accessed January 2016</ref> - Five statues of Buddha located on the western edge of the crater, built during the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}. Visitors come to pray for good relationships and safe childbirth. In 1663 (Kanbun 3), {{ill|Asakura Seibei|ja|朝倉清兵衛}}<ref name="odawaracity">Regarding Asakura Seibei, the on-site explanation board (viewed in 2018) describes him as a fishing boss. As a reference, [Odawara City Historical Materials](https://www.city.odawara.kanagawa.jp/field/lifelong/property/cultural/rekishishiryou/rekishichoukouzan.html) (January 2011) mentions a stonemason of the same name from {{ill|Iwamura, Kanagawa|ja|岩村}} who worked on the construction of Shōtaiji Temple in Kanbun 9 (1669).</ref>, a fishing boss from Iwamura in {{ill|Sagami Province|ja|相模国}} (now {{ill|Manazuru, Kanagawa|ja|真鶴町}}, Kanagawa Prefecture), prayed at Ōmuroyama Sengen Shrine for the safe childbirth of his 9-year-old daughter. In gratitude for her safe delivery, he erected these statues of the {{ill|Five Wisdom Buddhas|ja|五智如来}}<ref>On the on-site explanation board (viewed in 2018), the title reads "Gochi Nyorai Jizō-son," and the explanatory text mentions the creation of "Gochi Nyorai-zō." However, since the stone statues resemble Buddhas rather than Jizō, and because Nyorai-zō refers to carved stone figures, "Five Wisdom Buddha Statues" is used here.</ref>. They are said to be made of {{ill|Manazuru stone|ja|真鶴石}} and were transported from {{ill|Futo Port|ja|富戸港}}.
* '''Yatsugatake Jizō-son''' (八ケ岳地蔵尊)<ref name="yatsu">Source: [On-site explanation board](http://canned-seachicken.up.n.seesaa.net/canned-seachicken/image/E585ABE383B6E5B2B3E59CB0E894B5E5B08AE381AEE794B1E69DA5.jpg), accessed January 2016</ref> - Eight {{ill|Jizō|ja|地蔵}} statues located on the southern edge of the crater. They were erected by fishermen who used Ōmuroyama as a landmark, praying for maritime safety and good catches. There are both old and new statues, with the front ones built in 1984 (Shōwa 59) through donations from local residents of {{ill|Ike District|ja|池区}}.
<gallery> File:Itoh_20181124_115026.jpg|link=https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E3%83%95%E3%82%A1%E3%82%A4%E3%83%AB:Itoh_20181124_115026.jpg|Five Wisdom Buddha Statues File:Itoh_20181124_113718.jpg|link=https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E3%83%95%E3%82%A1%E3%82%A4%E3%83%AB:Itoh_20181124_113718.jpg|Yatsugatake Jizō-son File:Ōmuroyama_Sengen_Jinja_Haiden_and_Sessha.jpg|link=https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E3%83%95%E3%82%A1%E3%82%A4%E3%83%AB:%C5%8Cmuroyama_Sengen_Jinja_Haiden_and_Sessha.jpg|Ōmuroyama Sengen Shrine </gallery>

In 1663 (Kanbun 3), {{ill|Asakura Seibei|ja|朝倉清兵衛}}<ref name="odawaracity">Regarding Asakura Seibei, the on-site explanation board (viewed in 2018) describes him as a fishing boss. As a reference, [Odawara City Historical Materials](https://www.city.odawara.kanagawa.jp/field/lifelong/property/cultural/rekishishiryou/rekishichoukouzan.html) (January 2011) mentions a stonemason of the same name from {{ill|Iwamura, Kanagawa|ja|岩村}} who worked on the construction of Shōtaiji Temple in Kanbun 9 (1669).</ref>, a fishing boss from Iwamura in {{ill|Sagami Province|ja|相模国}} (now {{ill|Manazuru, Kanagawa|ja|真鶴町}}, Kanagawa Prefecture), prayed at Ōmuroyama Sengen Shrine for the safe childbirth of his 9-year-old daughter. In gratitude for her safe delivery, he erected these statues of the {{ill|Five Wisdom Buddhas|ja|五智如来}}<ref>On the on-site explanation board (viewed in 2018), the title reads "Gochi Nyorai Jizō-son," and the explanatory text mentions the creation of "Gochi Nyorai-zō." However, since the stone statues resemble Buddhas rather than Jizō, and because Nyorai-zō refers to carved stone figures, "Five Wisdom Buddha Statues" is used here.</ref>. They are said to be made of {{ill|Manazuru stone|ja|真鶴石}} and were transported from {{ill|Futo Port|ja|富戸港}}.
* '''Yatsugatake Jizō-son''' (八ケ岳地蔵尊)<ref name="yatsu">Source: [On-site explanation board](http://canned-seachicken.up.n.seesaa.net/canned-seachicken/image/E585ABE383B6E5B2B3E59CB0E894B5E5B08AE381AEE794B1E69DA5.jpg), accessed January 2016</ref> - Eight {{ill|Jizō|ja|地蔵}} statues located on the southern edge of the crater. They were erected by fishermen who used Ōmuroyama as a landmark, praying for maritime safety and good catches. There are both old and new statues, with the front ones built in 1984 (Shōwa 59) through donations from local residents of {{ill|Ike District|ja|池区}}.
<gallery> File:Itoh_20181124_115026.jpg|link=https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E3%83%95%E3%82%A1%E3%82%A4%E3%83%AB:Itoh_20181124_115026.jpg|Five Wisdom Buddha Statues File:Itoh_20181124_113718.jpg|link=https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E3%83%95%E3%82%A1%E3%82%A4%E3%83%AB:Itoh_20181124_113718.jpg|Yatsugatake Jizō-son File:Ōmuroyama_Sengen_Jinja_Haiden_and_Sessha.jpg|link=https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E3%83%95%E3%82%A1%E3%82%A4%E3%83%AB:%C5%8Cmuroyama_Sengen_Jinja_Haiden_and_Sessha.jpg|Ōmuroyama Sengen Shrine </gallery>

revision.text:
{{distinguish|{{ill|Omuroyama Sengen Shrine|ja|大室山浅間神社}}|{{ill|Omuro Sengen Shrine (Ōasomi, Fujiyoshida)|ja|小室浅間神社_(富士吉田市大明見)}}|{{ill|Fuji Omuro Asama Shrine|ja|冨士御室浅間神社}}}} [[File:Ōmuroyama Sengen Jinja Torii.jpg|thumb|the entrance torii]] Omuroyama Sengen Shrine (大室山浅間神社) is a Shrine for [[Iwanaga-hime]]. It is present on [[Mount Ōmuro (Shizuoka)|Mount Ōmuro]]
It has a wikimedia commons Category here [https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/Category%3A%C5%8Cmuroyama_Sengen_Jinja]
== Overview ==
Omuroyama Sengen Shrine is a Shinto shrine located within the crater of Mount Omuro in Ito City, Shizuoka Prefecture, Japan. Established in 1654 by Matsudaira Izumokami, the shrine is dedicated to the deities associated with safe childbirth and matchmaking.<ref>https://oneclick-onetrip-blog.com/en/shizuoka-prefecture-mt-omuro/?utm_source=chatgpt.com</ref>
Visitors can reach the summit of Mount Omuro, standing at 580 meters above sea level, via a six-minute chairlift ride. From there, a steep staircase near the lift station leads down into the crater where the shrine is situated. The crater also features an archery range, offering a unique experience for visitors.<ref>https://oneclick-onetrip-blog.com/en/shizuoka-prefecture-mt-omuro/?utm_source=chatgpt.com</ref>
The shrine is part of a broader tradition of Sengen shrines in Japan, which are typically dedicated to Konohanasakuya-hime, the deity associated with Mount Fuji and volcanoes. These shrines are often located near volcanic mountains and are linked to prayers for protection against eruptions and natural disasters.<ref>https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sengen_shrine?utm_source=chatgpt.com</ref>
Mount Omuro itself is a prominent feature of the Izu Peninsula, known for its distinctive bowl-shaped silhouette and lush greenery. The mountain offers panoramic views of the surrounding area, including sights of Mount Fuji, the Izu Islands, and Sagami Bay on clear days. An annual event called "Yamayaki," where the entire mountain is set ablaze, takes place on the second Sunday of February, attracting numerous tourists.<ref>https://savorjp.info/scenery/nature/6097/?utm_source=chatgpt.com</ref>
For travelers planning a visit, the area around Mount Omuro and Omuroyama Sengen Shrine offers various attractions, including the Izu Shaboten Zoo and scenic walking paths. Accommodations are available nearby, with several hotels and guesthouses catering to different preferences.<ref>https://www.tripadvisor.ca/HotelsNear-g298123-d10339157-Omuroyama_Sengen_Shrine-Ito_Shizuoka_Prefecture_Tokai_Chubu.html?utm_source=chatgpt.com</ref>
Experiencing Omuroyama Sengen Shrine provides insight into Japan's rich cultural heritage and offers a serene environment for reflection amidst natural beauty. [[ja:大室山浅間神社]]
== Mythology == Ōmuroyama Sengen Shrine is dedicated to Iwanaga hime, where she is worshipped through a [[Kannabi]] that represents her residing directly within the mountain as her [[shintai]].<ref name=":2">{{Cite web|title=パワースポット|url=https://omuroyama.com/event/powerspot/|access-date=2024-05-17|website=大室山登山リフトオフィシャルサイト}}</ref>
Omuroyama itself was once believed to be divine. Now houses Sengen Shrine on its mid slope near the crater.<ref name=":2" />
The shrine houses the deity Iwanaga hime no mikoto, known for her connection, to safe childbirth and matchmaking. She is the sister of Konohanasakuya hime the goddess revered at the peak of Mount Fuji.<ref name=":2" />
Located on the summit along a mountain path are five life sized Wisdom Buddhas (Gochi Nyorai). 300 years ago a nine year old girl, daughter of Asakura Seibee, an official from Iwamura in Sagami Province (now Ashigarakami District, Kanagawa Prefecture) had a safe delivery thanks to prayers offered at Omuroyama Sengen Shrine. In gratitude for this blessing these Buddhas were enshrined.<ref name=":2" />
In times local fishermen sought protection and safety from sea disasters, at Omuroyama facing Oshima Island. This led to the dedication of eight Jizo statues known as Yatsugatake Jizo at the summit of Omuroyama.<ref name=":2" />
At Omuroyama stands the haiku monument honoring Takaha Shugyo, who admired and cherished the Izu region alongside his master Akimoto Fujio.<ref name=":2" />
The haiku says, "Izu is where the sun gently falls, blooming with mandarin flowers " beautifully capturing the beauty of Izus landscape in the summer.<ref name=":2" />
== Shrines & Stone Buddhas ==
* '''Ōmuroyama Sengen Shrine''' (大室山浅間神社)<ref name="asama">Source: [On-site explanation board](http://itospa.com/sanpo/wp-content/uploads/2010/10/omuroyama101027-19.jpg), accessed January 2016</ref> - A shrine located on the eastern mid-slope within the crater. It enshrines {{ill|Iwanaga-hime no Mikoto|ja|磐長姫命}} as its sole deity. She is worshipped as a goddess of safe childbirth and matchmaking. While most {{ill|Sengen Shrines|ja|浅間神社}} primarily enshrine {{ill|Asama no Ōkami|ja|浅間大神}} and {{ill|Konohanasakuya-hime|ja|木花之佐久夜毘売}}, it is rare to find one that venerates Iwanaga-hime no Mikoto<ref name="Arakawa2008">{{Cite journal|Japanese|author=Rie Arakawa|title=The Spiritual Power of Ishinaga-hime|journal=Gakushuin University Studies in Ancient Literature|volume=33|date=March 31, 2008|pages=1-26|issn=0916-2453}}</ref>. Iwanaga-hime, the elder sister, and Konohanasakuya-hime, the younger sister, were both married to {{ill|Ninigi no Mikoto|ja|瓊々杵命}}, but the elder sister, considered unattractive, was sent back<ref>In the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}, Konohanasakuya-hime gives birth to the fire god in a burning birthing hut. However, according to the on-site explanation board (viewed in 2018), it is said that Iwanaga-hime, who was sent back, gave birth to the fire god in a burning thatched hut on Ōmuroyama.</ref>. Like Ōmuroyama, {{ill|Kumomi Sengen Shrine|ja|雲見浅間神社}} on Mount Eboshi (in {{ill|Matsuzaki, Shizuoka|ja|松崎町}}, Kamo District, Shizuoka Prefecture), an isolated peak with views of Mount Fuji, also enshrines the elder sister.
* '''Gochi Nyorai Jizō-son''' (五智如来地蔵尊)<ref name="gochi">Source: [On-site explanation board](http://obtweb.typepad.jp/.a/6a0120a877a494970b0192ac3acebb970d-800wi), accessed January 2016</ref> - Five statues of Buddha located on the western edge of the crater, built during the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}. Visitors come to pray for good relationships and safe childbirth. In 1663 (Kanbun 3), {{ill|Asakura Seibei|ja|朝倉清兵衛}}<ref name="odawaracity">Regarding Asakura Seibei, the on-site explanation board (viewed in 2018) describes him as a fishing boss. As a reference, [Odawara City Historical Materials](https://www.city.odawara.kanagawa.jp/field/lifelong/property/cultural/rekishishiryou/rekishichoukouzan.html) (January 2011) mentions a stonemason of the same name from {{ill|Iwamura, Kanagawa|ja|岩村}} who worked on the construction of Shōtaiji Temple in Kanbun 9 (1669).</ref>, a fishing boss from Iwamura in {{ill|Sagami Province|ja|相模国}} (now {{ill|Manazuru, Kanagawa|ja|真鶴町}}, Kanagawa Prefecture), prayed at Ōmuroyama Sengen Shrine for the safe childbirth of his 9-year-old daughter. In gratitude for her safe delivery, he erected these statues of the {{ill|Five Wisdom Buddhas|ja|五智如来}}<ref>On the on-site explanation board (viewed in 2018), the title reads "Gochi Nyorai Jizō-son," and the explanatory text mentions the creation of "Gochi Nyorai-zō." However, since the stone statues resemble Buddhas rather than Jizō, and because Nyorai-zō refers to carved stone figures, "Five Wisdom Buddha Statues" is used here.</ref>. They are said to be made of {{ill|Manazuru stone|ja|真鶴石}} and were transported from {{ill|Futo Port|ja|富戸港}}.
* '''Yatsugatake Jizō-son''' (八ケ岳地蔵尊)<ref name="yatsu">Source: [On-site explanation board](http://canned-seachicken.up.n.seesaa.net/canned-seachicken/image/E585ABE383B6E5B2B3E59CB0E894B5E5B08AE381AEE794B1E69DA5.jpg), accessed January 2016</ref> - Eight {{ill|Jizō|ja|地蔵}} statues located on the southern edge of the crater. They were erected by fishermen who used Ōmuroyama as a landmark, praying for maritime safety and good catches. There are both old and new statues, with the front ones built in 1984 (Shōwa 59) through donations from local residents of {{ill|Ike District|ja|池区}}.
<gallery> File:Itoh_20181124_115026.jpg|link=https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E3%83%95%E3%82%A1%E3%82%A4%E3%83%AB:Itoh_20181124_115026.jpg|Five Wisdom Buddha Statues File:Itoh_20181124_113718.jpg|link=https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E3%83%95%E3%82%A1%E3%82%A4%E3%83%AB:Itoh_20181124_113718.jpg|Yatsugatake Jizō-son File:Ōmuroyama_Sengen_Jinja_Haiden_and_Sessha.jpg|link=https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E3%83%95%E3%82%A1%E3%82%A4%E3%83%AB:%C5%8Cmuroyama_Sengen_Jinja_Haiden_and_Sessha.jpg|Ōmuroyama Sengen Shrine </gallery>

{{distinguish|{{ill|Omuroyama Sengen Shrine|ja|大室山浅間神社}}|{{ill|Omuro Sengen Shrine (Ōasomi, Fujiyoshida)|ja|小室浅間神社_(富士吉田市大明見)}}|{{ill|Fuji Omuro Asama Shrine|ja|冨士御室浅間神社}}}} [[File:Ōmuroyama Sengen Jinja Torii.jpg|thumb|the entrance torii]] Omuroyama Sengen Shrine (大室山浅間神社) is a Shrine for [[Iwanaga-hime]]. It is present on [[Mount Ōmuro (Shizuoka)|Mount Ōmuro]]
It has a wikimedia commons Category here [https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/Category%3A%C5%8Cmuroyama_Sengen_Jinja]
== Overview ==
Omuroyama Sengen Shrine is a Shinto shrine located within the crater of Mount Omuro in Ito City, Shizuoka Prefecture, Japan. Established in 1654 by Matsudaira Izumokami, the shrine is dedicated to the deities associated with safe childbirth and matchmaking.<ref>https://oneclick-onetrip-blog.com/en/shizuoka-prefecture-mt-omuro/?utm_source=chatgpt.com</ref>
Visitors can reach the summit of Mount Omuro, standing at 580 meters above sea level, via a six-minute chairlift ride. From there, a steep staircase near the lift station leads down into the crater where the shrine is situated. The crater also features an archery range, offering a unique experience for visitors.<ref>https://oneclick-onetrip-blog.com/en/shizuoka-prefecture-mt-omuro/?utm_source=chatgpt.com</ref>
The shrine is part of a broader tradition of Sengen shrines in Japan, which are typically dedicated to Konohanasakuya-hime, the deity associated with Mount Fuji and volcanoes. These shrines are often located near volcanic mountains and are linked to prayers for protection against eruptions and natural disasters.<ref>https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sengen_shrine?utm_source=chatgpt.com</ref>
Mount Omuro itself is a prominent feature of the Izu Peninsula, known for its distinctive bowl-shaped silhouette and lush greenery. The mountain offers panoramic views of the surrounding area, including sights of Mount Fuji, the Izu Islands, and Sagami Bay on clear days. An annual event called "Yamayaki," where the entire mountain is set ablaze, takes place on the second Sunday of February, attracting numerous tourists.<ref>https://savorjp.info/scenery/nature/6097/?utm_source=chatgpt.com</ref>
For travelers planning a visit, the area around Mount Omuro and Omuroyama Sengen Shrine offers various attractions, including the Izu Shaboten Zoo and scenic walking paths. Accommodations are available nearby, with several hotels and guesthouses catering to different preferences.<ref>https://www.tripadvisor.ca/HotelsNear-g298123-d10339157-Omuroyama_Sengen_Shrine-Ito_Shizuoka_Prefecture_Tokai_Chubu.html?utm_source=chatgpt.com</ref>
Experiencing Omuroyama Sengen Shrine provides insight into Japan's rich cultural heritage and offers a serene environment for reflection amidst natural beauty. [[ja:大室山浅間神社]]
== Mythology == Ōmuroyama Sengen Shrine is dedicated to Iwanaga hime, where she is worshipped through a [[Kannabi]] that represents her residing directly within the mountain as her [[shintai]].<ref name=":2">{{Cite web|title=パワースポット|url=https://omuroyama.com/event/powerspot/|access-date=2024-05-17|website=大室山登山リフトオフィシャルサイト}}</ref>
Omuroyama itself was once believed to be divine. Now houses Sengen Shrine on its mid slope near the crater.<ref name=":2" />
The shrine houses the deity Iwanaga hime no mikoto, known for her connection, to safe childbirth and matchmaking. She is the sister of Konohanasakuya hime the goddess revered at the peak of Mount Fuji.<ref name=":2" />
Located on the summit along a mountain path are five life sized Wisdom Buddhas (Gochi Nyorai). 300 years ago a nine year old girl, daughter of Asakura Seibee, an official from Iwamura in Sagami Province (now Ashigarakami District, Kanagawa Prefecture) had a safe delivery thanks to prayers offered at Omuroyama Sengen Shrine. In gratitude for this blessing these Buddhas were enshrined.<ref name=":2" />
In times local fishermen sought protection and safety from sea disasters, at Omuroyama facing Oshima Island. This led to the dedication of eight Jizo statues known as Yatsugatake Jizo at the summit of Omuroyama.<ref name=":2" />
At Omuroyama stands the haiku monument honoring Takaha Shugyo, who admired and cherished the Izu region alongside his master Akimoto Fujio.<ref name=":2" />
The haiku says, "Izu is where the sun gently falls, blooming with mandarin flowers " beautifully capturing the beauty of Izus landscape in the summer.<ref name=":2" />
== Shrines & Stone Buddhas ==
* '''Ōmuroyama Sengen Shrine''' (大室山浅間神社)<ref name="asama">Source: [On-site explanation board](http://itospa.com/sanpo/wp-content/uploads/2010/10/omuroyama101027-19.jpg), accessed January 2016</ref> - A shrine located on the eastern mid-slope within the crater. It enshrines {{ill|Iwanaga-hime no Mikoto|ja|磐長姫命}} as its sole deity. She is worshipped as a goddess of safe childbirth and matchmaking. While most {{ill|Sengen Shrines|ja|浅間神社}} primarily enshrine {{ill|Asama no Ōkami|ja|浅間大神}} and {{ill|Konohanasakuya-hime|ja|木花之佐久夜毘売}}, it is rare to find one that venerates Iwanaga-hime no Mikoto<ref name="Arakawa2008">{{Cite journal|Japanese|author=Rie Arakawa|title=The Spiritual Power of Ishinaga-hime|journal=Gakushuin University Studies in Ancient Literature|volume=33|date=March 31, 2008|pages=1-26|issn=0916-2453}}</ref>. Iwanaga-hime, the elder sister, and Konohanasakuya-hime, the younger sister, were both married to {{ill|Ninigi no Mikoto|ja|瓊々杵命}}, but the elder sister, considered unattractive, was sent back<ref>In the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}, Konohanasakuya-hime gives birth to the fire god in a burning birthing hut. However, according to the on-site explanation board (viewed in 2018), it is said that Iwanaga-hime, who was sent back, gave birth to the fire god in a burning thatched hut on Ōmuroyama.</ref>. Like Ōmuroyama, {{ill|Kumomi Sengen Shrine|ja|雲見浅間神社}} on Mount Eboshi (in {{ill|Matsuzaki, Shizuoka|ja|松崎町}}, Kamo District, Shizuoka Prefecture), an isolated peak with views of Mount Fuji, also enshrines the elder sister.
* '''Gochi Nyorai Jizō-son''' (五智如来地蔵尊)<ref name="gochi">Source: [On-site explanation board](http://obtweb.typepad.jp/.a/6a0120a877a494970b0192ac3acebb970d-800wi), accessed January 2016</ref> - Five statues of Buddha located on the western edge of the crater, built during the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}. Visitors come to pray for good relationships and safe childbirth. In 1663 (Kanbun 3), {{ill|Asakura Seibei|ja|朝倉清兵衛}}<ref name="odawaracity">Regarding Asakura Seibei, the on-site explanation board (viewed in 2018) describes him as a fishing boss. As a reference, [Odawara City Historical Materials](https://www.city.odawara.kanagawa.jp/field/lifelong/property/cultural/rekishishiryou/rekishichoukouzan.html) (January 2011) mentions a stonemason of the same name from {{ill|Iwamura, Kanagawa|ja|岩村}} who worked on the construction of Shōtaiji Temple in Kanbun 9 (1669).</ref>, a fishing boss from Iwamura in {{ill|Sagami Province|ja|相模国}} (now {{ill|Manazuru, Kanagawa|ja|真鶴町}}, Kanagawa Prefecture), prayed at Ōmuroyama Sengen Shrine for the safe childbirth of his 9-year-old daughter. In gratitude for her safe delivery, he erected these statues of the {{ill|Five Wisdom Buddhas|ja|五智如来}}<ref>On the on-site explanation board (viewed in 2018), the title reads "Gochi Nyorai Jizō-son," and the explanatory text mentions the creation of "Gochi Nyorai-zō." However, since the stone statues resemble Buddhas rather than Jizō, and because Nyorai-zō refers to carved stone figures, "Five Wisdom Buddha Statues" is used here.</ref>. They are said to be made of {{ill|Manazuru stone|ja|真鶴石}} and were transported from {{ill|Futo Port|ja|富戸港}}.
* '''Yatsugatake Jizō-son''' (八ケ岳地蔵尊)<ref name="yatsu">Source: [On-site explanation board](http://canned-seachicken.up.n.seesaa.net/canned-seachicken/image/E585ABE383B6E5B2B3E59CB0E894B5E5B08AE381AEE794B1E69DA5.jpg), accessed January 2016</ref> - Eight {{ill|Jizō|ja|地蔵}} statues located on the southern edge of the crater. They were erected by fishermen who used Ōmuroyama as a landmark, praying for maritime safety and good catches. There are both old and new statues, with the front ones built in 1984 (Shōwa 59) through donations from local residents of {{ill|Ike District|ja|池区}}.
<gallery> File:Itoh_20181124_115026.jpg|link=https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E3%83%95%E3%82%A1%E3%82%A4%E3%83%AB:Itoh_20181124_115026.jpg|Five Wisdom Buddha Statues File:Itoh_20181124_113718.jpg|link=https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E3%83%95%E3%82%A1%E3%82%A4%E3%83%AB:Itoh_20181124_113718.jpg|Yatsugatake Jizō-son File:Ōmuroyama_Sengen_Jinja_Haiden_and_Sessha.jpg|link=https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E3%83%95%E3%82%A1%E3%82%A4%E3%83%AB:%C5%8Cmuroyama_Sengen_Jinja_Haiden_and_Sessha.jpg|Ōmuroyama Sengen Shrine </gallery>
