{{distinguish|{{ill|Omuroyama Sengen Shrine|ja|大室山浅間神社}}|{{ill|Omuro Sengen Shrine (Ōasomi, Fujiyoshida)|ja|小室浅間神社_(富士吉田市大明見)}}|{{ill|Fuji Omuro Asama Shrine|ja|冨士御室浅間神社}}}}[[File:Mount_Omuroyama_Shrine_from_other_side_of_crater.jpg|thumb|View of the shrine that clearly shows its position in the crater]][[File:Ōmuroyama Sengen Jinja Torii.jpg|thumb|the entrance torii]] Omuroyama Sengen Shrine (大室山浅間神社) is a Shrine for [[Iwanaga-hime]]. It is present on [[Mount Ōmuro (Shizuoka)|Mount Ōmuro]]
It has a wikimedia commons Category here [https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/Category%3A%C5%8Cmuroyama_Sengen_Jinja]
== Overview ==
Omuroyama Sengen Shrine is a Shinto shrine located within the crater of Mount Omuro in Ito City, Shizuoka Prefecture, Japan. Established in 1654 by Matsudaira Izumokami, the shrine is dedicated to the deities associated with safe childbirth and matchmaking.<ref>https://oneclick-onetrip-blog.com/en/shizuoka-prefecture-mt-omuro/</ref>
Visitors can reach the summit of Mount Omuro, standing at 580 meters above sea level, via a six-minute chairlift ride. From there, a steep staircase near the lift station leads down into the crater where the shrine is situated. The crater also features an archery range, offering a unique experience for visitors.<ref>https://oneclick-onetrip-blog.com/en/shizuoka-prefecture-mt-omuro/</ref>
The shrine is part of a broader tradition of Sengen shrines in Japan, which are typically dedicated to Konohanasakuya-hime, the deity associated with Mount Fuji and volcanoes. These shrines are often located near volcanic mountains and are linked to prayers for protection against eruptions and natural disasters.<ref>https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sengen_shrine</ref>
Mount Omuro itself is a prominent feature of the Izu Peninsula, known for its distinctive bowl-shaped silhouette and lush greenery. The mountain offers panoramic views of the surrounding area, including sights of Mount Fuji, the Izu Islands, and Sagami Bay on clear days. An annual event called "Yamayaki," where the entire mountain is set ablaze, takes place on the second Sunday of February, attracting numerous tourists.<ref>https://savorjp.info/scenery/nature/6097/</ref>
For travelers planning a visit, the area around Mount Omuro and Omuroyama Sengen Shrine offers various attractions, including the Izu Shaboten Zoo and scenic walking paths. Accommodations are available nearby, with several hotels and guesthouses catering to different preferences.<ref>https://www.tripadvisor.ca/HotelsNear-g298123-d10339157-Omuroyama_Sengen_Shrine-Ito_Shizuoka_Prefecture_Tokai_Chubu.html</ref>
Experiencing Omuroyama Sengen Shrine provides insight into Japan's rich cultural heritage and offers a serene environment for reflection amidst natural beauty.
== Mythology == Ōmuroyama Sengen Shrine is dedicated to Iwanaga hime, where she is worshipped through a [[Kannabi]] that represents her residing directly within the mountain as her [[shintai]].<ref name=":2">{{Cite web|title=パワースポット|url=https://omuroyama.com/event/powerspot/|access-date=2024-05-17|website=大室山登山リフトオフィシャルサイト}}</ref>
Omuroyama itself was once believed to be divine. Now houses Sengen Shrine on its mid slope near the crater.<ref name=":2" />
The shrine houses the deity Iwanaga hime no mikoto, known for her connection, to safe childbirth and matchmaking. She is the sister of Konohanasakuya hime the goddess revered at the peak of Mount Fuji.<ref name=":2" />
Located on the summit along a mountain path are five life sized Wisdom Buddhas (Gochi Nyorai). 300 years ago a nine year old girl, daughter of Asakura Seibee, an official from Iwamura in Sagami Province (now Ashigarakami District, Kanagawa Prefecture) had a safe delivery thanks to prayers offered at Omuroyama Sengen Shrine. In gratitude for this blessing these Buddhas were enshrined.<ref name=":2" />
In times local fishermen sought protection and safety from sea disasters, at Omuroyama facing Oshima Island. This led to the dedication of eight Jizo statues known as Yatsugatake Jizo at the summit of Omuroyama.<ref name=":2" />
At Omuroyama stands the haiku monument honoring Takaha Shugyo, who admired and cherished the Izu region alongside his master Akimoto Fujio.<ref name=":2" />
The haiku says, "Izu is where the sun gently falls, blooming with mandarin flowers " beautifully capturing the beauty of Izus landscape in the summer.<ref name=":2" />
== Shrines & Stone Buddhas ==
* '''Ōmuroyama Sengen Shrine''' (大室山浅間神社)<ref name="asama">Source: [On-site explanation board](http://itospa.com/sanpo/wp-content/uploads/2010/10/omuroyama101027-19.jpg), accessed January 2016</ref> - A shrine located on the eastern mid-slope within the crater. It enshrines {{ill|Iwanaga-hime no Mikoto|ja|磐長姫命|1=Iwanaga-hime}} as its sole deity. She is worshipped as a goddess of safe childbirth and matchmaking. While most {{ill|Sengen Shrines|ja|浅間神社}} primarily enshrine {{ill|Asama no Ōkami|ja|浅間大神}} and {{ill|Konohanasakuya-hime|ja|木花之佐久夜毘売}}, it is rare to find one that venerates Iwanaga-hime no Mikoto<ref name="Arakawa2008">{{Cite journal|Japanese|author=Rie Arakawa|title=The Spiritual Power of Ishinaga-hime|journal=Gakushuin University Studies in Ancient Literature|volume=33|date=March 31, 2008|pages=1-26|issn=0916-2453}}</ref>. Iwanaga-hime, the elder sister, and Konohanasakuya-hime, the younger sister, were both married to {{ill|Ninigi no Mikoto|ja|瓊々杵命}}, but the elder sister, considered unattractive, was sent back<ref>In the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}, Konohanasakuya-hime gives birth to the fire god in a burning birthing hut. However, according to the on-site explanation board (viewed in 2018), it is said that Iwanaga-hime, who was sent back, gave birth to the fire god in a burning thatched hut on Ōmuroyama.</ref>. Like Ōmuroyama, {{ill|Kumomi Sengen Shrine|ja|雲見浅間神社}} on Mount Eboshi (in {{ill|Matsuzaki, Shizuoka|ja|松崎町}}, Kamo District, Shizuoka Prefecture), an isolated peak with views of Mount Fuji, also enshrines the elder sister.
* '''Gochi Nyorai Jizō-son''' (五智如来地蔵尊)<ref name="gochi">Source: [On-site explanation board](http://obtweb.typepad.jp/.a/6a0120a877a494970b0192ac3acebb970d-800wi), accessed January 2016</ref> - Five statues of Buddha located on the western edge of the crater, built during the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}. Visitors come to pray for good relationships and safe childbirth. In 1663 (Kanbun 3), {{ill|Asakura Seibei|ja|朝倉清兵衛}}<ref name="odawaracity">Regarding Asakura Seibei, the on-site explanation board (viewed in 2018) describes him as a fishing boss. As a reference, [Odawara City Historical Materials](https://www.city.odawara.kanagawa.jp/field/lifelong/property/cultural/rekishishiryou/rekishichoukouzan.html) (January 2011) mentions a stonemason of the same name from {{ill|Iwamura, Kanagawa|ja|岩村}} who worked on the construction of Shōtaiji Temple in Kanbun 9 (1669).</ref>, a fishing boss from Iwamura in {{ill|Sagami Province|ja|相模国}} (now {{ill|Manazuru, Kanagawa|ja|真鶴町}}, Kanagawa Prefecture), prayed at Ōmuroyama Sengen Shrine for the safe childbirth of his 9-year-old daughter. In gratitude for her safe delivery, he erected these statues of the {{ill|Five Wisdom Buddhas|ja|五智如来}}<ref>On the on-site explanation board (viewed in 2018), the title reads "Gochi Nyorai Jizō-son," and the explanatory text mentions the creation of "Gochi Nyorai-zō." However, since the stone statues resemble Buddhas rather than Jizō, and because Nyorai-zō refers to carved stone figures, "Five Wisdom Buddha Statues" is used here.</ref>. They are said to be made of {{ill|Manazuru stone|ja|真鶴石}} and were transported from {{ill|Futo Port|ja|富戸港}}.
* '''Yatsugatake Jizō-son''' (八ケ岳地蔵尊)<ref name="yatsu">Source: [On-site explanation board](http://canned-seachicken.up.n.seesaa.net/canned-seachicken/image/E585ABE383B6E5B2B3E59CB0E894B5E5B08AE381AEE794B1E69DA5.jpg), accessed January 2016</ref> - Eight {{ill|Jizō|ja|地蔵}} statues located on the southern edge of the crater. They were erected by fishermen who used Ōmuroyama as a landmark, praying for maritime safety and good catches. There are both old and new statues, with the front ones built in 1984 (Shōwa 59) through donations from local residents of {{ill|Ike District|ja|池区}}.
<gallery> File:Itoh_20181124_115026.jpg|link=https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E3%83%95%E3%82%A1%E3%82%A4%E3%83%AB:Itoh_20181124_115026.jpg|Five Wisdom Buddha Statues File:Itoh_20181124_113718.jpg|link=https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E3%83%95%E3%82%A1%E3%82%A4%E3%83%AB:Itoh_20181124_113718.jpg|Yatsugatake Jizō-son File:Ōmuroyama_Sengen_Jinja_Haiden_and_Sessha.jpg|link=https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E3%83%95%E3%82%A1%E3%82%A4%E3%83%AB:%C5%8Cmuroyama_Sengen_Jinja_Haiden_and_Sessha.jpg|Ōmuroyama Sengen Shrine </gallery>

Established in 1654 by Matsudaira Izumokami, the shrine is dedicated to the deities associated with safe childbirth and matchmaking.<ref>https://oneclick-onetrip-blog.com/en/shizuoka-prefecture-mt-omuro/</ref>

These shrines are often located near volcanic mountains and are linked to prayers for protection against eruptions and natural disasters.<ref>https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sengen_shrine</ref>

In gratitude for this blessing these Buddhas were enshrined.<ref name=":2" />
In times local fishermen sought protection and safety from sea disasters, at Omuroyama facing Oshima Island. This led to the dedication of eight Jizo statues known as Yatsugatake Jizo at the summit of Omuroyama.<ref name=":2" />
At Omuroyama stands the haiku monument honoring Takaha Shugyo, who admired and cherished the Izu region alongside his master Akimoto Fujio.<ref name=":2" />
The haiku says, "Izu is where the sun gently falls, blooming with mandarin flowers " beautifully capturing the beauty of Izus landscape in the summer.<ref name=":2" />
== Shrines & Stone Buddhas ==
* '''Ōmuroyama Sengen Shrine''' (大室山浅間神社)<ref name="asama">Source: [On-site explanation board](http://itospa.com/sanpo/wp-content/uploads/2010/10/omuroyama101027-19.jpg), accessed January 2016</ref> - A shrine located on the eastern mid-slope within the crater. It enshrines {{ill|Iwanaga-hime no Mikoto|ja|磐長姫命|1=Iwanaga-hime|12=draft}} as its sole deity. She is worshipped as a goddess of safe childbirth and matchmaking. While most {{ill|Sengen Shrines|ja|浅間神社|12=draft}} primarily enshrine {{ill|Asama no Ōkami|ja|浅間大神|12=draft}} and {{ill|Konohanasakuya-hime|ja|木花之佐久夜毘売|12=draft}}, it is rare to find one that venerates Iwanaga-hime no Mikoto<ref name="Arakawa2008">{{Cite journal|Japanese|author=Rie Arakawa|title=The Spiritual Power of Ishinaga-hime|journal=Gakushuin University Studies in Ancient Literature|volume=33|date=March 31, 2008|pages=1-26|issn=0916-2453}}</ref>. Iwanaga-hime, the elder sister, and Konohanasakuya-hime, the younger sister, were both married to {{ill|Ninigi no Mikoto|ja|瓊々杵命|12=draft}}, but the elder sister, considered unattractive, was sent back<ref>In the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記|12=draft}}, Konohanasakuya-hime gives birth to the fire god in a burning birthing hut. However, according to the on-site explanation board (viewed in 2018), it is said that Iwanaga-hime, who was sent back, gave birth to the fire god in a burning thatched hut on Ōmuroyama.</ref>. Like Ōmuroyama, {{ill|Kumomi Sengen Shrine|ja|雲見浅間神社|12=draft}} on Mount Eboshi (in {{ill|Matsuzaki, Shizuoka|ja|松崎町|12=draft}}, Kamo District, Shizuoka Prefecture), an isolated peak with views of Mount Fuji, also enshrines the elder sister.
* '''Gochi Nyorai Jizō-son''' (五智如来地蔵尊)<ref name="gochi">Source: [On-site explanation board](http://obtweb.typepad.jp/.a/6a0120a877a494970b0192ac3acebb970d-800wi), accessed January 2016</ref> - Five statues of Buddha located on the western edge of the crater, built during the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代|12=draft}}. Visitors come to pray for good relationships and safe childbirth. In 1663 (Kanbun 3), {{ill|Asakura Seibei|ja|朝倉清兵衛|12=draft}}<ref name="odawaracity">Regarding Asakura Seibei, the on-site explanation board (viewed in 2018) describes him as a fishing boss. As a reference, [Odawara City Historical Materials](https://www.city.odawara.kanagawa.jp/field/lifelong/property/cultural/rekishishiryou/rekishichoukouzan.html) (January 2011) mentions a stonemason of the same name from {{ill|Iwamura, Kanagawa|ja|岩村|12=draft}} who worked on the construction of Shōtaiji Temple in Kanbun 9 (1669).</ref>, a fishing boss from Iwamura in {{ill|Sagami Province|ja|相模国|12=draft}} (now {{ill|Manazuru, Kanagawa|ja|真鶴町|12=draft}}, Kanagawa Prefecture), prayed at Ōmuroyama Sengen Shrine for the safe childbirth of his 9-year-old daughter. In gratitude for her safe delivery, he erected these statues of the {{ill|Five Wisdom Buddhas|ja|五智如来|12=draft}}<ref>On the on-site explanation board (viewed in 2018), the title reads "Gochi Nyorai Jizō-son," and the explanatory text mentions the creation of "Gochi Nyorai-zō." However, since the stone statues resemble Buddhas rather than Jizō, and because Nyorai-zō refers to carved stone figures, "Five Wisdom Buddha Statues" is used here.</ref>. They are said to be made of {{ill|Manazuru stone|ja|真鶴石|12=draft}} and were transported from {{ill|Futo Port|ja|富戸港|12=draft}}.
* '''Yatsugatake Jizō-son''' (八ケ岳地蔵尊)<ref name="yatsu">Source: [On-site explanation board](http://canned-seachicken.up.n.seesaa.net/canned-seachicken/image/E585ABE383B6E5B2B3E59CB0E894B5E5B08AE381AEE794B1E69DA5.jpg), accessed January 2016</ref> - Eight {{ill|Jizō|ja|地蔵|12=draft}} statues located on the southern edge of the crater. They were erected by fishermen who used Ōmuroyama as a landmark, praying for maritime safety and good catches. There are both old and new statues, with the front ones built in 1984 (Shōwa 59) through donations from local residents of {{ill|Ike District|ja|池区|12=draft}}.

revision.text:
{{distinguish|{{ill|Omuroyama Sengen Shrine|ja|大室山浅間神社|12=draft}}|{{ill|Omuro Sengen Shrine (Ōasomi, Fujiyoshida)|ja|小室浅間神社_(富士吉田市大明見)|12=draft}}|{{ill|Fuji Omuro Asama Shrine|ja|冨士御室浅間神社|12=draft}}}}[[File:Mount_Omuroyama_Shrine_from_other_side_of_crater.jpg|thumb|View of the shrine that clearly shows its position in the crater]][[File:Ōmuroyama Sengen Jinja Torii.jpg|thumb|the entrance torii]] Omuroyama Sengen Shrine (大室山浅間神社) is a Shrine for [[Iwanaga-hime]]. It is present on [[Mount Ōmuro (Shizuoka)|Mount Ōmuro]]
It has a wikimedia commons Category here [https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/Category%3A%C5%8Cmuroyama_Sengen_Jinja]
== Overview ==
Omuroyama Sengen Shrine is a Shinto shrine located within the crater of Mount Omuro in Ito City, Shizuoka Prefecture, Japan. Established in 1654 by Matsudaira Izumokami, the shrine is dedicated to the deities associated with safe childbirth and matchmaking.<ref>https://oneclick-onetrip-blog.com/en/shizuoka-prefecture-mt-omuro/</ref>
Visitors can reach the summit of Mount Omuro, standing at 580 meters above sea level, via a six-minute chairlift ride. From there, a steep staircase near the lift station leads down into the crater where the shrine is situated. The crater also features an archery range, offering a unique experience for visitors.<ref>https://oneclick-onetrip-blog.com/en/shizuoka-prefecture-mt-omuro/</ref>
The shrine is part of a broader tradition of Sengen shrines in Japan, which are typically dedicated to Konohanasakuya-hime, the deity associated with Mount Fuji and volcanoes. These shrines are often located near volcanic mountains and are linked to prayers for protection against eruptions and natural disasters.<ref>https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sengen_shrine</ref>
Mount Omuro itself is a prominent feature of the Izu Peninsula, known for its distinctive bowl-shaped silhouette and lush greenery. The mountain offers panoramic views of the surrounding area, including sights of Mount Fuji, the Izu Islands, and Sagami Bay on clear days. An annual event called "Yamayaki," where the entire mountain is set ablaze, takes place on the second Sunday of February, attracting numerous tourists.<ref>https://savorjp.info/scenery/nature/6097/</ref>
For travelers planning a visit, the area around Mount Omuro and Omuroyama Sengen Shrine offers various attractions, including the Izu Shaboten Zoo and scenic walking paths. Accommodations are available nearby, with several hotels and guesthouses catering to different preferences.<ref>https://www.tripadvisor.ca/HotelsNear-g298123-d10339157-Omuroyama_Sengen_Shrine-Ito_Shizuoka_Prefecture_Tokai_Chubu.html</ref>
Experiencing Omuroyama Sengen Shrine provides insight into Japan's rich cultural heritage and offers a serene environment for reflection amidst natural beauty.
== Mythology == Ōmuroyama Sengen Shrine is dedicated to Iwanaga hime, where she is worshipped through a [[Kannabi]] that represents her residing directly within the mountain as her [[shintai]].<ref name=":2">{{Cite web|title=パワースポット|url=https://omuroyama.com/event/powerspot/|access-date=2024-05-17|website=大室山登山リフトオフィシャルサイト}}</ref>
Omuroyama itself was once believed to be divine. Now houses Sengen Shrine on its mid slope near the crater.<ref name=":2" />
The shrine houses the deity Iwanaga hime no mikoto, known for her connection, to safe childbirth and matchmaking. She is the sister of Konohanasakuya hime the goddess revered at the peak of Mount Fuji.<ref name=":2" />
Located on the summit along a mountain path are five life sized Wisdom Buddhas (Gochi Nyorai). 300 years ago a nine year old girl, daughter of Asakura Seibee, an official from Iwamura in Sagami Province (now Ashigarakami District, Kanagawa Prefecture) had a safe delivery thanks to prayers offered at Omuroyama Sengen Shrine. In gratitude for this blessing these Buddhas were enshrined.<ref name=":2" />
In times local fishermen sought protection and safety from sea disasters, at Omuroyama facing Oshima Island. This led to the dedication of eight Jizo statues known as Yatsugatake Jizo at the summit of Omuroyama.<ref name=":2" />
At Omuroyama stands the haiku monument honoring Takaha Shugyo, who admired and cherished the Izu region alongside his master Akimoto Fujio.<ref name=":2" />
The haiku says, "Izu is where the sun gently falls, blooming with mandarin flowers " beautifully capturing the beauty of Izus landscape in the summer.<ref name=":2" />
== Shrines & Stone Buddhas ==
* '''Ōmuroyama Sengen Shrine''' (大室山浅間神社)<ref name="asama">Source: [On-site explanation board](http://itospa.com/sanpo/wp-content/uploads/2010/10/omuroyama101027-19.jpg), accessed January 2016</ref> - A shrine located on the eastern mid-slope within the crater. It enshrines {{ill|Iwanaga-hime no Mikoto|ja|磐長姫命|1=Iwanaga-hime|12=draft}} as its sole deity. She is worshipped as a goddess of safe childbirth and matchmaking. While most {{ill|Sengen Shrines|ja|浅間神社|12=draft}} primarily enshrine {{ill|Asama no Ōkami|ja|浅間大神|12=draft}} and {{ill|Konohanasakuya-hime|ja|木花之佐久夜毘売|12=draft}}, it is rare to find one that venerates Iwanaga-hime no Mikoto<ref name="Arakawa2008">{{Cite journal|Japanese|author=Rie Arakawa|title=The Spiritual Power of Ishinaga-hime|journal=Gakushuin University Studies in Ancient Literature|volume=33|date=March 31, 2008|pages=1-26|issn=0916-2453}}</ref>. Iwanaga-hime, the elder sister, and Konohanasakuya-hime, the younger sister, were both married to {{ill|Ninigi no Mikoto|ja|瓊々杵命|12=draft}}, but the elder sister, considered unattractive, was sent back<ref>In the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記|12=draft}}, Konohanasakuya-hime gives birth to the fire god in a burning birthing hut. However, according to the on-site explanation board (viewed in 2018), it is said that Iwanaga-hime, who was sent back, gave birth to the fire god in a burning thatched hut on Ōmuroyama.</ref>. Like Ōmuroyama, {{ill|Kumomi Sengen Shrine|ja|雲見浅間神社|12=draft}} on Mount Eboshi (in {{ill|Matsuzaki, Shizuoka|ja|松崎町|12=draft}}, Kamo District, Shizuoka Prefecture), an isolated peak with views of Mount Fuji, also enshrines the elder sister.
* '''Gochi Nyorai Jizō-son''' (五智如来地蔵尊)<ref name="gochi">Source: [On-site explanation board](http://obtweb.typepad.jp/.a/6a0120a877a494970b0192ac3acebb970d-800wi), accessed January 2016</ref> - Five statues of Buddha located on the western edge of the crater, built during the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代|12=draft}}. Visitors come to pray for good relationships and safe childbirth. In 1663 (Kanbun 3), {{ill|Asakura Seibei|ja|朝倉清兵衛|12=draft}}<ref name="odawaracity">Regarding Asakura Seibei, the on-site explanation board (viewed in 2018) describes him as a fishing boss. As a reference, [Odawara City Historical Materials](https://www.city.odawara.kanagawa.jp/field/lifelong/property/cultural/rekishishiryou/rekishichoukouzan.html) (January 2011) mentions a stonemason of the same name from {{ill|Iwamura, Kanagawa|ja|岩村|12=draft}} who worked on the construction of Shōtaiji Temple in Kanbun 9 (1669).</ref>, a fishing boss from Iwamura in {{ill|Sagami Province|ja|相模国|12=draft}} (now {{ill|Manazuru, Kanagawa|ja|真鶴町|12=draft}}, Kanagawa Prefecture), prayed at Ōmuroyama Sengen Shrine for the safe childbirth of his 9-year-old daughter. In gratitude for her safe delivery, he erected these statues of the {{ill|Five Wisdom Buddhas|ja|五智如来|12=draft}}<ref>On the on-site explanation board (viewed in 2018), the title reads "Gochi Nyorai Jizō-son," and the explanatory text mentions the creation of "Gochi Nyorai-zō." However, since the stone statues resemble Buddhas rather than Jizō, and because Nyorai-zō refers to carved stone figures, "Five Wisdom Buddha Statues" is used here.</ref>. They are said to be made of {{ill|Manazuru stone|ja|真鶴石|12=draft}} and were transported from {{ill|Futo Port|ja|富戸港|12=draft}}.
* '''Yatsugatake Jizō-son''' (八ケ岳地蔵尊)<ref name="yatsu">Source: [On-site explanation board](http://canned-seachicken.up.n.seesaa.net/canned-seachicken/image/E585ABE383B6E5B2B3E59CB0E894B5E5B08AE381AEE794B1E69DA5.jpg), accessed January 2016</ref> - Eight {{ill|Jizō|ja|地蔵|12=draft}} statues located on the southern edge of the crater. They were erected by fishermen who used Ōmuroyama as a landmark, praying for maritime safety and good catches. There are both old and new statues, with the front ones built in 1984 (Shōwa 59) through donations from local residents of {{ill|Ike District|ja|池区|12=draft}}.

{{distinguish|{{ill|Omuroyama Sengen Shrine|ja|大室山浅間神社|12=draft}}|{{ill|Omuro Sengen Shrine (Ōasomi, Fujiyoshida)|ja|小室浅間神社_(富士吉田市大明見)|12=draft}}|{{ill|Fuji Omuro Asama Shrine|ja|冨士御室浅間神社|12=draft}}}}[[File:Mount_Omuroyama_Shrine_from_other_side_of_crater.jpg|thumb|View of the shrine that clearly shows its position in the crater]][[File:Ōmuroyama Sengen Jinja Torii.jpg|thumb|the entrance torii]] Omuroyama Sengen Shrine (大室山浅間神社) is a Shrine for [[Iwanaga-hime]]. It is present on [[Mount Ōmuro (Shizuoka)|Mount Ōmuro]]
It has a wikimedia commons Category here [https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/Category%3A%C5%8Cmuroyama_Sengen_Jinja]
== Overview ==
Omuroyama Sengen Shrine is a Shinto shrine located within the crater of Mount Omuro in Ito City, Shizuoka Prefecture, Japan. Established in 1654 by Matsudaira Izumokami, the shrine is dedicated to the deities associated with safe childbirth and matchmaking.<ref>https://oneclick-onetrip-blog.com/en/shizuoka-prefecture-mt-omuro/</ref>
Visitors can reach the summit of Mount Omuro, standing at 580 meters above sea level, via a six-minute chairlift ride. From there, a steep staircase near the lift station leads down into the crater where the shrine is situated. The crater also features an archery range, offering a unique experience for visitors.<ref>https://oneclick-onetrip-blog.com/en/shizuoka-prefecture-mt-omuro/</ref>
The shrine is part of a broader tradition of Sengen shrines in Japan, which are typically dedicated to Konohanasakuya-hime, the deity associated with Mount Fuji and volcanoes. These shrines are often located near volcanic mountains and are linked to prayers for protection against eruptions and natural disasters.<ref>https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sengen_shrine</ref>
Mount Omuro itself is a prominent feature of the Izu Peninsula, known for its distinctive bowl-shaped silhouette and lush greenery. The mountain offers panoramic views of the surrounding area, including sights of Mount Fuji, the Izu Islands, and Sagami Bay on clear days. An annual event called "Yamayaki," where the entire mountain is set ablaze, takes place on the second Sunday of February, attracting numerous tourists.<ref>https://savorjp.info/scenery/nature/6097/</ref>
For travelers planning a visit, the area around Mount Omuro and Omuroyama Sengen Shrine offers various attractions, including the Izu Shaboten Zoo and scenic walking paths. Accommodations are available nearby, with several hotels and guesthouses catering to different preferences.<ref>https://www.tripadvisor.ca/HotelsNear-g298123-d10339157-Omuroyama_Sengen_Shrine-Ito_Shizuoka_Prefecture_Tokai_Chubu.html</ref>
Experiencing Omuroyama Sengen Shrine provides insight into Japan's rich cultural heritage and offers a serene environment for reflection amidst natural beauty.
== Mythology == Ōmuroyama Sengen Shrine is dedicated to Iwanaga hime, where she is worshipped through a [[Kannabi]] that represents her residing directly within the mountain as her [[shintai]].<ref name=":2">{{Cite web|title=パワースポット|url=https://omuroyama.com/event/powerspot/|access-date=2024-05-17|website=大室山登山リフトオフィシャルサイト}}</ref>
Omuroyama itself was once believed to be divine. Now houses Sengen Shrine on its mid slope near the crater.<ref name=":2" />
The shrine houses the deity Iwanaga hime no mikoto, known for her connection, to safe childbirth and matchmaking. She is the sister of Konohanasakuya hime the goddess revered at the peak of Mount Fuji.<ref name=":2" />
Located on the summit along a mountain path are five life sized Wisdom Buddhas (Gochi Nyorai). 300 years ago a nine year old girl, daughter of Asakura Seibee, an official from Iwamura in Sagami Province (now Ashigarakami District, Kanagawa Prefecture) had a safe delivery thanks to prayers offered at Omuroyama Sengen Shrine. In gratitude for this blessing these Buddhas were enshrined.<ref name=":2" />
In times local fishermen sought protection and safety from sea disasters, at Omuroyama facing Oshima Island. This led to the dedication of eight Jizo statues known as Yatsugatake Jizo at the summit of Omuroyama.<ref name=":2" />
At Omuroyama stands the haiku monument honoring Takaha Shugyo, who admired and cherished the Izu region alongside his master Akimoto Fujio.<ref name=":2" />
The haiku says, "Izu is where the sun gently falls, blooming with mandarin flowers " beautifully capturing the beauty of Izus landscape in the summer.<ref name=":2" />
== Shrines & Stone Buddhas ==
* '''Ōmuroyama Sengen Shrine''' (大室山浅間神社)<ref name="asama">Source: [On-site explanation board](http://itospa.com/sanpo/wp-content/uploads/2010/10/omuroyama101027-19.jpg), accessed January 2016</ref> - A shrine located on the eastern mid-slope within the crater. It enshrines {{ill|Iwanaga-hime no Mikoto|ja|磐長姫命|1=Iwanaga-hime|12=draft}} as its sole deity. She is worshipped as a goddess of safe childbirth and matchmaking. While most {{ill|Sengen Shrines|ja|浅間神社|12=draft}} primarily enshrine {{ill|Asama no Ōkami|ja|浅間大神|12=draft}} and {{ill|Konohanasakuya-hime|ja|木花之佐久夜毘売|12=draft}}, it is rare to find one that venerates Iwanaga-hime no Mikoto<ref name="Arakawa2008">{{Cite journal|Japanese|author=Rie Arakawa|title=The Spiritual Power of Ishinaga-hime|journal=Gakushuin University Studies in Ancient Literature|volume=33|date=March 31, 2008|pages=1-26|issn=0916-2453}}</ref>. Iwanaga-hime, the elder sister, and Konohanasakuya-hime, the younger sister, were both married to {{ill|Ninigi no Mikoto|ja|瓊々杵命|12=draft}}, but the elder sister, considered unattractive, was sent back<ref>In the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記|12=draft}}, Konohanasakuya-hime gives birth to the fire god in a burning birthing hut. However, according to the on-site explanation board (viewed in 2018), it is said that Iwanaga-hime, who was sent back, gave birth to the fire god in a burning thatched hut on Ōmuroyama.</ref>. Like Ōmuroyama, {{ill|Kumomi Sengen Shrine|ja|雲見浅間神社|12=draft}} on Mount Eboshi (in {{ill|Matsuzaki, Shizuoka|ja|松崎町|12=draft}}, Kamo District, Shizuoka Prefecture), an isolated peak with views of Mount Fuji, also enshrines the elder sister.
* '''Gochi Nyorai Jizō-son''' (五智如来地蔵尊)<ref name="gochi">Source: [On-site explanation board](http://obtweb.typepad.jp/.a/6a0120a877a494970b0192ac3acebb970d-800wi), accessed January 2016</ref> - Five statues of Buddha located on the western edge of the crater, built during the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代|12=draft}}. Visitors come to pray for good relationships and safe childbirth. In 1663 (Kanbun 3), {{ill|Asakura Seibei|ja|朝倉清兵衛|12=draft}}<ref name="odawaracity">Regarding Asakura Seibei, the on-site explanation board (viewed in 2018) describes him as a fishing boss. As a reference, [Odawara City Historical Materials](https://www.city.odawara.kanagawa.jp/field/lifelong/property/cultural/rekishishiryou/rekishichoukouzan.html) (January 2011) mentions a stonemason of the same name from {{ill|Iwamura, Kanagawa|ja|岩村|12=draft}} who worked on the construction of Shōtaiji Temple in Kanbun 9 (1669).</ref>, a fishing boss from Iwamura in {{ill|Sagami Province|ja|相模国|12=draft}} (now {{ill|Manazuru, Kanagawa|ja|真鶴町|12=draft}}, Kanagawa Prefecture), prayed at Ōmuroyama Sengen Shrine for the safe childbirth of his 9-year-old daughter. In gratitude for her safe delivery, he erected these statues of the {{ill|Five Wisdom Buddhas|ja|五智如来|12=draft}}<ref>On the on-site explanation board (viewed in 2018), the title reads "Gochi Nyorai Jizō-son," and the explanatory text mentions the creation of "Gochi Nyorai-zō." However, since the stone statues resemble Buddhas rather than Jizō, and because Nyorai-zō refers to carved stone figures, "Five Wisdom Buddha Statues" is used here.</ref>. They are said to be made of {{ill|Manazuru stone|ja|真鶴石|12=draft}} and were transported from {{ill|Futo Port|ja|富戸港|12=draft}}.
* '''Yatsugatake Jizō-son''' (八ケ岳地蔵尊)<ref name="yatsu">Source: [On-site explanation board](http://canned-seachicken.up.n.seesaa.net/canned-seachicken/image/E585ABE383B6E5B2B3E59CB0E894B5E5B08AE381AEE794B1E69DA5.jpg), accessed January 2016</ref> - Eight {{ill|Jizō|ja|地蔵|12=draft}} statues located on the southern edge of the crater. They were erected by fishermen who used Ōmuroyama as a landmark, praying for maritime safety and good catches. There are both old and new statues, with the front ones built in 1984 (Shōwa 59) through donations from local residents of {{ill|Ike District|ja|池区|12=draft}}.
<gallery> File:Itoh_20181124_115026.jpg|link=https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E3%83%95%E3%82%A1%E3%82%A4%E3%83%AB:Itoh_20181124_115026.jpg|Five Wisdom Buddha Statues File:Itoh_20181124_113718.jpg|link=https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E3%83%95%E3%82%A1%E3%82%A4%E3%83%AB:Itoh_20181124_113718.jpg|Yatsugatake Jizō-son File:Ōmuroyama_Sengen_Jinja_Haiden_and_Sessha.jpg|link=https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E3%83%95%E3%82%A1%E3%82%A4%E3%83%AB:%C5%8Cmuroyama_Sengen_Jinja_Haiden_and_Sessha.jpg|Ōmuroyama Sengen Shrine </gallery>

They were erected by fishermen who used Ōmuroyama as a landmark, praying for maritime safety and good catches. There are both old and new statues, with the front ones built in 1984 (Shōwa 59) through donations from local residents of {{ill|Ike District|ja|池区|12=draft}}.

{{distinguish|{{ill|Omuroyama Sengen Shrine|ja|大室山浅間神社|qq=draft}}|{{ill|Omuro Sengen Shrine (Ōasomi, Fujiyoshida)|ja|小室浅間神社_(富士吉田市大明見)|qq=draft}}|{{ill|Fuji Omuro Asama Shrine|ja|冨士御室浅間神社|qq=draft}}}}[[File:Mount_Omuroyama_Shrine_from_other_side_of_crater.jpg|thumb|View of the shrine that clearly shows its position in the crater]][[File:Ōmuroyama Sengen Jinja Torii.jpg|thumb|the entrance torii]] Omuroyama Sengen Shrine (大室山浅間神社) is a Shrine for [[Iwanaga-hime]]. It is present on [[Mount Ōmuro (Shizuoka)|Mount Ōmuro]]
It has a wikimedia commons Category here [https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/Category%3A%C5%8Cmuroyama_Sengen_Jinja]
== Overview ==
Omuroyama Sengen Shrine is a Shinto shrine located within the crater of Mount Omuro in Ito City, Shizuoka Prefecture, Japan. Established in 1654 by Matsudaira Izumokami, the shrine is dedicated to the deities associated with safe childbirth and matchmaking.<ref>https://oneclick-onetrip-blog.com/en/shizuoka-prefecture-mt-omuro/</ref>
Visitors can reach the summit of Mount Omuro, standing at 580 meters above sea level, via a six-minute chairlift ride. From there, a steep staircase near the lift station leads down into the crater where the shrine is situated. The crater also features an archery range, offering a unique experience for visitors.<ref>https://oneclick-onetrip-blog.com/en/shizuoka-prefecture-mt-omuro/</ref>
The shrine is part of a broader tradition of Sengen shrines in Japan, which are typically dedicated to Konohanasakuya-hime, the deity associated with Mount Fuji and volcanoes. These shrines are often located near volcanic mountains and are linked to prayers for protection against eruptions and natural disasters.<ref>https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sengen_shrine</ref>
Mount Omuro itself is a prominent feature of the Izu Peninsula, known for its distinctive bowl-shaped silhouette and lush greenery. The mountain offers panoramic views of the surrounding area, including sights of Mount Fuji, the Izu Islands, and Sagami Bay on clear days. An annual event called "Yamayaki," where the entire mountain is set ablaze, takes place on the second Sunday of February, attracting numerous tourists.<ref>https://savorjp.info/scenery/nature/6097/</ref>
For travelers planning a visit, the area around Mount Omuro and Omuroyama Sengen Shrine offers various attractions, including the Izu Shaboten Zoo and scenic walking paths. Accommodations are available nearby, with several hotels and guesthouses catering to different preferences.<ref>https://www.tripadvisor.ca/HotelsNear-g298123-d10339157-Omuroyama_Sengen_Shrine-Ito_Shizuoka_Prefecture_Tokai_Chubu.html</ref>
Experiencing Omuroyama Sengen Shrine provides insight into Japan's rich cultural heritage and offers a serene environment for reflection amidst natural beauty.
== Mythology == Ōmuroyama Sengen Shrine is dedicated to Iwanaga hime, where she is worshipped through a [[Kannabi]] that represents her residing directly within the mountain as her [[shintai]].<ref name=":2">{{Cite web|title=パワースポット|url=https://omuroyama.com/event/powerspot/|access-date=2024-05-17|website=大室山登山リフトオフィシャルサイト}}</ref>
Omuroyama itself was once believed to be divine. Now houses Sengen Shrine on its mid slope near the crater.<ref name=":2" />
The shrine houses the deity Iwanaga hime no mikoto, known for her connection, to safe childbirth and matchmaking. She is the sister of Konohanasakuya hime the goddess revered at the peak of Mount Fuji.<ref name=":2" />
Located on the summit along a mountain path are five life sized Wisdom Buddhas (Gochi Nyorai). 300 years ago a nine year old girl, daughter of Asakura Seibee, an official from Iwamura in Sagami Province (now Ashigarakami District, Kanagawa Prefecture) had a safe delivery thanks to prayers offered at Omuroyama Sengen Shrine. In gratitude for this blessing these Buddhas were enshrined.<ref name=":2" />
In times local fishermen sought protection and safety from sea disasters, at Omuroyama facing Oshima Island. This led to the dedication of eight Jizo statues known as Yatsugatake Jizo at the summit of Omuroyama.<ref name=":2" />
At Omuroyama stands the haiku monument honoring Takaha Shugyo, who admired and cherished the Izu region alongside his master Akimoto Fujio.<ref name=":2" />
The haiku says, "Izu is where the sun gently falls, blooming with mandarin flowers " beautifully capturing the beauty of Izus landscape in the summer.<ref name=":2" />
== Shrines & Stone Buddhas ==
* '''Ōmuroyama Sengen Shrine''' (大室山浅間神社)<ref name="asama">Source: [On-site explanation board](http://itospa.com/sanpo/wp-content/uploads/2010/10/omuroyama101027-19.jpg), accessed January 2016</ref> - A shrine located on the eastern mid-slope within the crater. It enshrines {{ill|Iwanaga-hime no Mikoto|ja|磐長姫命|1=Iwanaga-hime|qq=draft}} as its sole deity. She is worshipped as a goddess of safe childbirth and matchmaking. While most {{ill|Sengen Shrines|ja|浅間神社|qq=draft}} primarily enshrine {{ill|Asama no Ōkami|ja|浅間大神|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Konohanasakuya-hime|ja|木花之佐久夜毘売|qq=draft}}, it is rare to find one that venerates Iwanaga-hime no Mikoto<ref name="Arakawa2008">{{Cite journal|Japanese|author=Rie Arakawa|title=The Spiritual Power of Ishinaga-hime|journal=Gakushuin University Studies in Ancient Literature|volume=33|date=March 31, 2008|pages=1-26|issn=0916-2453}}</ref>. Iwanaga-hime, the elder sister, and Konohanasakuya-hime, the younger sister, were both married to {{ill|Ninigi no Mikoto|ja|瓊々杵命|qq=draft}}, but the elder sister, considered unattractive, was sent back<ref>In the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記|qq=draft}}, Konohanasakuya-hime gives birth to the fire god in a burning birthing hut. However, according to the on-site explanation board (viewed in 2018), it is said that Iwanaga-hime, who was sent back, gave birth to the fire god in a burning thatched hut on Ōmuroyama.</ref>. Like Ōmuroyama, {{ill|Kumomi Sengen Shrine|ja|雲見浅間神社|qq=draft}} on Mount Eboshi (in {{ill|Matsuzaki, Shizuoka|ja|松崎町|qq=draft}}, Kamo District, Shizuoka Prefecture), an isolated peak with views of Mount Fuji, also enshrines the elder sister.
* '''Gochi Nyorai Jizō-son''' (五智如来地蔵尊)<ref name="gochi">Source: [On-site explanation board](http://obtweb.typepad.jp/.a/6a0120a877a494970b0192ac3acebb970d-800wi), accessed January 2016</ref> - Five statues of Buddha located on the western edge of the crater, built during the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代|qq=draft}}. Visitors come to pray for good relationships and safe childbirth. In 1663 (Kanbun 3), {{ill|Asakura Seibei|ja|朝倉清兵衛|qq=draft}}<ref name="odawaracity">Regarding Asakura Seibei, the on-site explanation board (viewed in 2018) describes him as a fishing boss. As a reference, [Odawara City Historical Materials](https://www.city.odawara.kanagawa.jp/field/lifelong/property/cultural/rekishishiryou/rekishichoukouzan.html) (January 2011) mentions a stonemason of the same name from {{ill|Iwamura, Kanagawa|ja|岩村|qq=draft}} who worked on the construction of Shōtaiji Temple in Kanbun 9 (1669).</ref>, a fishing boss from Iwamura in {{ill|Sagami Province|ja|相模国|qq=draft}} (now {{ill|Manazuru, Kanagawa|ja|真鶴町|qq=draft}}, Kanagawa Prefecture), prayed at Ōmuroyama Sengen Shrine for the safe childbirth of his 9-year-old daughter. In gratitude for her safe delivery, he erected these statues of the {{ill|Five Wisdom Buddhas|ja|五智如来|qq=draft}}<ref>On the on-site explanation board (viewed in 2018), the title reads "Gochi Nyorai Jizō-son," and the explanatory text mentions the creation of "Gochi Nyorai-zō." However, since the stone statues resemble Buddhas rather than Jizō, and because Nyorai-zō refers to carved stone figures, "Five Wisdom Buddha Statues" is used here.</ref>. They are said to be made of {{ill|Manazuru stone|ja|真鶴石|qq=draft}} and were transported from {{ill|Futo Port|ja|富戸港|qq=draft}}.
* '''Yatsugatake Jizō-son''' (八ケ岳地蔵尊)<ref name="yatsu">Source: [On-site explanation board](http://canned-seachicken.up.n.seesaa.net/canned-seachicken/image/E585ABE383B6E5B2B3E59CB0E894B5E5B08AE381AEE794B1E69DA5.jpg), accessed January 2016</ref> - Eight {{ill|Jizō|ja|地蔵|qq=draft}} statues located on the southern edge of the crater. They were erected by fishermen who used Ōmuroyama as a landmark, praying for maritime safety and good catches. There are both old and new statues, with the front ones built in 1984 (Shōwa 59) through donations from local residents of {{ill|Ike District|ja|池区|qq=draft}}.
<gallery> File:Itoh_20181124_115026.jpg|link=https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E3%83%95%E3%82%A1%E3%82%A4%E3%83%AB:Itoh_20181124_115026.jpg|Five Wisdom Buddha Statues File:Itoh_20181124_113718.jpg|link=https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E3%83%95%E3%82%A1%E3%82%A4%E3%83%AB:Itoh_20181124_113718.jpg|Yatsugatake Jizō-son File:Ōmuroyama_Sengen_Jinja_Haiden_and_Sessha.jpg|link=https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E3%83%95%E3%82%A1%E3%82%A4%E3%83%AB:%C5%8Cmuroyama_Sengen_Jinja_Haiden_and_Sessha.jpg|Ōmuroyama Sengen Shrine </gallery>

== Overview ==
Omuroyama Sengen Shrine is a Shinto shrine located within the crater of Mount Omuro in Ito City, Shizuoka Prefecture, Japan. Established in 1654 by Matsudaira Izumokami, the shrine is dedicated to the deities associated with safe childbirth and matchmaking.<ref>https://oneclick-onetrip-blog.com/en/shizuoka-prefecture-mt-omuro/</ref>
Visitors can reach the summit of Mount Omuro, standing at 580 meters above sea level, via a six-minute chairlift ride. From there, a steep staircase near the lift station leads down into the crater where the shrine is situated. The crater also features an archery range, offering a unique experience for visitors.<ref>https://oneclick-onetrip-blog.com/en/shizuoka-prefecture-mt-omuro/</ref>
The shrine is part of a broader tradition of Sengen shrines in Japan, which are typically dedicated to Konohanasakuya-hime, the deity associated with Mount Fuji and volcanoes. These shrines are often located near volcanic mountains and are linked to prayers for protection against eruptions and natural disasters.<ref>https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sengen_shrine</ref>
Mount Omuro itself is a prominent feature of the Izu Peninsula, known for its distinctive bowl-shaped silhouette and lush greenery. The mountain offers panoramic views of the surrounding area, including sights of Mount Fuji, the Izu Islands, and Sagami Bay on clear days. An annual event called "Yamayaki," where the entire mountain is set ablaze, takes place on the second Sunday of February, attracting numerous tourists.<ref>https://savorjp.info/scenery/nature/6097/</ref>
For travelers planning a visit, the area around Mount Omuro and Omuroyama Sengen Shrine offers various attractions, including the Izu Shaboten Zoo and scenic walking paths. Experiencing Omuroyama Sengen Shrine provides insight into Japan's rich cultural heritage and offers a serene environment for reflection amidst natural beauty.
== Mythology == Ōmuroyama Sengen Shrine is dedicated to Iwanaga hime, where she is worshipped through a [[Kannabi]] that represents her residing directly within the mountain as her [[shintai]].<ref name=":2">{{Cite web|title=パワースポット|url=https://omuroyama.com/event/powerspot/|access-date=2024-05-17|website=大室山登山リフトオフィシャルサイト}}</ref>
Omuroyama itself was once believed to be divine. Now houses Sengen Shrine on its mid slope near the crater.<ref name=":2" />
The shrine houses the deity Iwanaga hime no mikoto, known for her connection, to safe childbirth and matchmaking. She is the sister of Konohanasakuya hime the goddess revered at the peak of Mount Fuji.<ref name=":2" />
Located on the summit along a mountain path are five life sized Wisdom Buddhas (Gochi Nyorai). 300 years ago a nine year old girl, daughter of Asakura Seibee, an official from Iwamura in Sagami Province (now Ashigarakami District, Kanagawa Prefecture) had a safe delivery thanks to prayers offered at Omuroyama Sengen Shrine. In gratitude for this blessing these Buddhas were enshrined.<ref name=":2" />
In times local fishermen sought protection and safety from sea disasters, at Omuroyama facing Oshima Island. This led to the dedication of eight Jizo statues known as Yatsugatake Jizo at the summit of Omuroyama.<ref name=":2" />
At Omuroyama stands the haiku monument honoring Takaha Shugyo, who admired and cherished the Izu region alongside his master Akimoto Fujio.<ref name=":2" />
The haiku says, "Izu is where the sun gently falls, blooming with mandarin flowers " beautifully capturing the beauty of Izus landscape in the summer.<ref name=":2" />
== Shrines & Stone Buddhas ==
* '''Ōmuroyama Sengen Shrine''' (大室山浅間神社)<ref name="asama">Source: [On-site explanation board](http://itospa.com/sanpo/wp-content/uploads/2010/10/omuroyama101027-19.jpg), accessed January 2016</ref> - A shrine located on the eastern mid-slope within the crater. It enshrines {{ill|Iwanaga-hime no Mikoto|ja|磐長姫命|1=Iwanaga-hime|qq=draft}} as its sole deity. She is worshipped as a goddess of safe childbirth and matchmaking. While most {{ill|Sengen Shrines|ja|浅間神社|qq=draft}} primarily enshrine {{ill|Asama no Ōkami|ja|浅間大神|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Konohanasakuya-hime|ja|木花之佐久夜毘売|qq=draft}}, it is rare to find one that venerates Iwanaga-hime no Mikoto<ref name="Arakawa2008">{{Cite journal|Japanese|author=Rie Arakawa|title=The Spiritual Power of Ishinaga-hime|journal=Gakushuin University Studies in Ancient Literature|volume=33|date=March 31, 2008|pages=1-26|issn=0916-2453}}</ref>. Iwanaga-hime, the elder sister, and Konohanasakuya-hime, the younger sister, were both married to {{ill|Ninigi no Mikoto|ja|瓊々杵命|qq=draft}}, but the elder sister, considered unattractive, was sent back<ref>In the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記|qq=draft}}, Konohanasakuya-hime gives birth to the fire god in a burning birthing hut. However, according to the on-site explanation board (viewed in 2018), it is said that Iwanaga-hime, who was sent back, gave birth to the fire god in a burning thatched hut on Ōmuroyama.</ref>. Like Ōmuroyama, {{ill|Kumomi Sengen Shrine|ja|雲見浅間神社|qq=draft}} on Mount Eboshi (in {{ill|Matsuzaki, Shizuoka|ja|松崎町|qq=draft}}, Kamo District, Shizuoka Prefecture), an isolated peak with views of Mount Fuji, also enshrines the elder sister.
* '''Gochi Nyorai Jizō-son''' (五智如来地蔵尊)<ref name="gochi">Source: [On-site explanation board](http://obtweb.typepad.jp/.a/6a0120a877a494970b0192ac3acebb970d-800wi), accessed January 2016</ref> - Five statues of Buddha located on the western edge of the crater, built during the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代|qq=draft}}. Visitors come to pray for good relationships and safe childbirth. In 1663 (Kanbun 3), {{ill|Asakura Seibei|ja|朝倉清兵衛|qq=draft}}<ref name="odawaracity">Regarding Asakura Seibei, the on-site explanation board (viewed in 2018) describes him as a fishing boss. As a reference, [Odawara City Historical Materials](https://www.city.odawara.kanagawa.jp/field/lifelong/property/cultural/rekishishiryou/rekishichoukouzan.html) (January 2011) mentions a stonemason of the same name from {{ill|Iwamura, Kanagawa|ja|岩村|qq=draft}} who worked on the construction of Shōtaiji Temple in Kanbun 9 (1669).</ref>, a fishing boss from Iwamura in {{ill|Sagami Province|ja|相模国|qq=draft}} (now {{ill|Manazuru, Kanagawa|ja|真鶴町|qq=draft}}, Kanagawa Prefecture), prayed at Ōmuroyama Sengen Shrine for the safe childbirth of his 9-year-old daughter. In gratitude for her safe delivery, he erected these statues of the {{ill|Five Wisdom Buddhas|ja|五智如来|qq=draft}}<ref>On the on-site explanation board (viewed in 2018), the title reads "Gochi Nyorai Jizō-son," and the explanatory text mentions the creation of "Gochi Nyorai-zō." However, since the stone statues resemble Buddhas rather than Jizō, and because Nyorai-zō refers to carved stone figures, "Five Wisdom Buddha Statues" is used here.</ref>. They are said to be made of {{ill|Manazuru stone|ja|真鶴石|qq=draft}} and were transported from {{ill|Futo Port|ja|富戸港|qq=draft}}.
* '''Yatsugatake Jizō-son''' (八ケ岳地蔵尊)<ref name="yatsu">Source: [On-site explanation board](http://canned-seachicken.up.n.seesaa.net/canned-seachicken/image/E585ABE383B6E5B2B3E59CB0E894B5E5B08AE381AEE794B1E69DA5.jpg), accessed January 2016</ref> - Eight {{ill|Jizō|ja|地蔵|qq=draft}} statues located on the southern edge of the crater. They were erected by fishermen who used Ōmuroyama as a landmark, praying for maritime safety and good catches. There are both old and new statues, with the front ones built in 1984 (Shōwa 59) through donations from local residents of {{ill|Ike District|ja|池区|qq=draft}}.
<gallery> File:Itoh_20181124_115026.jpg|link=https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E3%83%95%E3%82%A1%E3%82%A4%E3%83%AB:Itoh_20181124_115026.jpg|Five Wisdom Buddha Statues File:Itoh_20181124_113718.jpg|link=https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E3%83%95%E3%82%A1%E3%82%A4%E3%83%AB:Itoh_20181124_113718.jpg|Yatsugatake Jizō-son File:Ōmuroyama_Sengen_Jinja_Haiden_and_Sessha.jpg|link=https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E3%83%95%E3%82%A1%E3%82%A4%E3%83%AB:%C5%8Cmuroyama_Sengen_Jinja_Haiden_and_Sessha.jpg|Ōmuroyama Sengen Shrine </gallery>

{{distinguish|{{ill|Omuroyama Sengen Shrine|ja|大室山浅間神社|qq=draft}}|{{ill|Omuro Sengen Shrine (Ōasomi, Fujiyoshida)|ja|小室浅間神社_(富士吉田市大明見)|qq=draft}}|{{ill|Fuji Omuro Asama Shrine|ja|冨士御室浅間神社|qq=draft}}}}[[File:Mount_Omuroyama_Shrine_from_other_side_of_crater.jpg|thumb|View of the shrine that clearly shows its position in the crater]][[File:Ōmuroyama Sengen Jinja Torii.jpg|thumb|the entrance torii]] Omuroyama Sengen Shrine (大室山浅間神社) is a Shrine for [[Iwanaga-hime]]. It is present on [[Mount Ōmuro (Shizuoka)|Mount Ōmuro]]
It has a wikimedia commons Category here [https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/Category%3A%C5%8Cmuroyama_Sengen_Jinja]
== Overview ==
Omuroyama Sengen Shrine is a Shinto shrine located within the crater of Mount Omuro in Ito City, Shizuoka Prefecture, Japan. Established in 1654 by Matsudaira Izumokami, the shrine is dedicated to the deities associated with safe childbirth and matchmaking.<ref>https://oneclick-onetrip-blog.com/en/shizuoka-prefecture-mt-omuro/</ref>
Visitors can reach the summit of Mount Omuro, standing at 580 meters above sea level, via a six-minute chairlift ride. From there, a steep staircase near the lift station leads down into the crater where the shrine is situated. The crater also features an archery range, offering a unique experience for visitors.<ref>https://oneclick-onetrip-blog.com/en/shizuoka-prefecture-mt-omuro/</ref>
The shrine is part of a broader tradition of Sengen shrines in Japan, which are typically dedicated to Konohanasakuya-hime, the deity associated with Mount Fuji and volcanoes. These shrines are often located near volcanic mountains and are linked to prayers for protection against eruptions and natural disasters.<ref>https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sengen_shrine</ref>
Mount Omuro itself is a prominent feature of the Izu Peninsula, known for its distinctive bowl-shaped silhouette and lush greenery. The mountain offers panoramic views of the surrounding area, including sights of Mount Fuji, the Izu Islands, and Sagami Bay on clear days. An annual event called "Yamayaki," where the entire mountain is set ablaze, takes place on the second Sunday of February, attracting numerous tourists.<ref>https://savorjp.info/scenery/nature/6097/</ref>
For travelers planning a visit, the area around Mount Omuro and Omuroyama Sengen Shrine offers various attractions, including the Izu Shaboten Zoo and scenic walking paths. Accommodations are available nearby, with several hotels and guesthouses catering to different preferences.<ref>https://www.tripadvisor.ca/HotelsNear-g298123-d10339157-Omuroyama_Sengen_Shrine-Ito_Shizuoka_Prefecture_Tokai_Chubu.html</ref>
Experiencing Omuroyama Sengen Shrine provides insight into Japan's rich cultural heritage and offers a serene environment for reflection amidst natural beauty.
== Mythology == Ōmuroyama Sengen Shrine is dedicated to Iwanaga hime, where she is worshipped through a [[Kannabi]] that represents her residing directly within the mountain as her [[shintai]].<ref name=":2">{{Cite web|title=パワースポット|url=https://omuroyama.com/event/powerspot/|access-date=2024-05-17|website=大室山登山リフトオフィシャルサイト}}</ref>
Omuroyama itself was once believed to be divine. Now houses Sengen Shrine on its mid slope near the crater.<ref name=":2" />
The shrine houses the deity Iwanaga hime no mikoto, known for her connection, to safe childbirth and matchmaking. She is the sister of Konohanasakuya hime the goddess revered at the peak of Mount Fuji.<ref name=":2" />
Located on the summit along a mountain path are five life sized Wisdom Buddhas (Gochi Nyorai). 300 years ago a nine year old girl, daughter of Asakura Seibee, an official from Iwamura in Sagami Province (now Ashigarakami District, Kanagawa Prefecture) had a safe delivery thanks to prayers offered at Omuroyama Sengen Shrine. In gratitude for this blessing these Buddhas were enshrined.<ref name=":2" />
In times local fishermen sought protection and safety from sea disasters, at Omuroyama facing Oshima Island. This led to the dedication of eight Jizo statues known as Yatsugatake Jizo at the summit of Omuroyama.<ref name=":2" />
At Omuroyama stands the haiku monument honoring Takaha Shugyo, who admired and cherished the Izu region alongside his master Akimoto Fujio.<ref name=":2" />
The haiku says, "Izu is where the sun gently falls, blooming with mandarin flowers " beautifully capturing the beauty of Izus landscape in the summer.<ref name=":2" />

revision.comment: Removing from [[Category:Pages needing history import]] using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
{{distinguish|{{ill|Omuroyama Sengen Shrine|ja|大室山浅間神社|qq=draft}}|{{ill|Omuro Sengen Shrine (Ōasomi, Fujiyoshida)|ja|小室浅間神社_(富士吉田市大明見)|qq=draft}}|{{ill|Fuji Omuro Asama Shrine|ja|冨士御室浅間神社|qq=draft}}}}[[File:Mount_Omuroyama_Shrine_from_other_side_of_crater.jpg|thumb|View of the shrine that clearly shows its position in the crater]][[File:Ōmuroyama Sengen Jinja Torii.jpg|thumb|the entrance torii]] Omuroyama Sengen Shrine (大室山浅間神社) is a Shrine for [[Iwanaga-hime]]. It is present on [[Mount Ōmuro (Shizuoka)|Mount Ōmuro]]
It has a wikimedia commons Category here [https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/Category%3A%C5%8Cmuroyama_Sengen_Jinja]
== Overview ==
Omuroyama Sengen Shrine is a Shinto shrine located within the crater of Mount Omuro in Ito City, Shizuoka Prefecture, Japan. Established in 1654 by Matsudaira Izumokami, the shrine is dedicated to the deities associated with safe childbirth and matchmaking.<ref>https://oneclick-onetrip-blog.com/en/shizuoka-prefecture-mt-omuro/</ref>
Visitors can reach the summit of Mount Omuro, standing at 580 meters above sea level, via a six-minute chairlift ride. From there, a steep staircase near the lift station leads down into the crater where the shrine is situated. The crater also features an archery range, offering a unique experience for visitors.<ref>https://oneclick-onetrip-blog.com/en/shizuoka-prefecture-mt-omuro/</ref>
The shrine is part of a broader tradition of Sengen shrines in Japan, which are typically dedicated to Konohanasakuya-hime, the deity associated with Mount Fuji and volcanoes. These shrines are often located near volcanic mountains and are linked to prayers for protection against eruptions and natural disasters.<ref>https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sengen_shrine</ref>
Mount Omuro itself is a prominent feature of the Izu Peninsula, known for its distinctive bowl-shaped silhouette and lush greenery. The mountain offers panoramic views of the surrounding area, including sights of Mount Fuji, the Izu Islands, and Sagami Bay on clear days. An annual event called "Yamayaki," where the entire mountain is set ablaze, takes place on the second Sunday of February, attracting numerous tourists.<ref>https://savorjp.info/scenery/nature/6097/</ref>
For travelers planning a visit, the area around Mount Omuro and Omuroyama Sengen Shrine offers various attractions, including the Izu Shaboten Zoo and scenic walking paths. Accommodations are available nearby, with several hotels and guesthouses catering to different preferences.<ref>https://www.tripadvisor.ca/HotelsNear-g298123-d10339157-Omuroyama_Sengen_Shrine-Ito_Shizuoka_Prefecture_Tokai_Chubu.html</ref>
Experiencing Omuroyama Sengen Shrine provides insight into Japan's rich cultural heritage and offers a serene environment for reflection amidst natural beauty.
== Mythology == Ōmuroyama Sengen Shrine is dedicated to Iwanaga hime, where she is worshipped through a [[Kannabi]] that represents her residing directly within the mountain as her [[shintai]].<ref name=":2">{{Cite web|title=パワースポット|url=https://omuroyama.com/event/powerspot/|access-date=2024-05-17|website=大室山登山リフトオフィシャルサイト}}</ref>
Omuroyama itself was once believed to be divine. Now houses Sengen Shrine on its mid slope near the crater.<ref name=":2" />
The shrine houses the deity Iwanaga hime no mikoto, known for her connection, to safe childbirth and matchmaking. She is the sister of Konohanasakuya hime the goddess revered at the peak of Mount Fuji.<ref name=":2" />
Located on the summit along a mountain path are five life sized Wisdom Buddhas (Gochi Nyorai). 300 years ago a nine year old girl, daughter of Asakura Seibee, an official from Iwamura in Sagami Province (now Ashigarakami District, Kanagawa Prefecture) had a safe delivery thanks to prayers offered at Omuroyama Sengen Shrine. In gratitude for this blessing these Buddhas were enshrined.<ref name=":2" />
In times local fishermen sought protection and safety from sea disasters, at Omuroyama facing Oshima Island. This led to the dedication of eight Jizo statues known as Yatsugatake Jizo at the summit of Omuroyama.<ref name=":2" />
At Omuroyama stands the haiku monument honoring Takaha Shugyo, who admired and cherished the Izu region alongside his master Akimoto Fujio.<ref name=":2" />
The haiku says, "Izu is where the sun gently falls, blooming with mandarin flowers " beautifully capturing the beauty of Izus landscape in the summer.<ref name=":2" />
== Shrines & Stone Buddhas ==
* '''Ōmuroyama Sengen Shrine''' (大室山浅間神社)<ref name="asama">Source: [On-site explanation board](http://itospa.com/sanpo/wp-content/uploads/2010/10/omuroyama101027-19.jpg), accessed January 2016</ref> - A shrine located on the eastern mid-slope within the crater. It enshrines {{ill|Iwanaga-hime no Mikoto|ja|磐長姫命|1=Iwanaga-hime|qq=draft}} as its sole deity. She is worshipped as a goddess of safe childbirth and matchmaking. While most {{ill|Sengen Shrines|ja|浅間神社|qq=draft}} primarily enshrine {{ill|Asama no Ōkami|ja|浅間大神|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Konohanasakuya-hime|ja|木花之佐久夜毘売|qq=draft}}, it is rare to find one that venerates Iwanaga-hime no Mikoto<ref name="Arakawa2008">{{Cite journal|Japanese|author=Rie Arakawa|title=The Spiritual Power of Ishinaga-hime|journal=Gakushuin University Studies in Ancient Literature|volume=33|date=March 31, 2008|pages=1-26|issn=0916-2453}}</ref>. Iwanaga-hime, the elder sister, and Konohanasakuya-hime, the younger sister, were both married to {{ill|Ninigi no Mikoto|ja|瓊々杵命|qq=draft}}, but the elder sister, considered unattractive, was sent back<ref>In the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記|qq=draft}}, Konohanasakuya-hime gives birth to the fire god in a burning birthing hut. However, according to the on-site explanation board (viewed in 2018), it is said that Iwanaga-hime, who was sent back, gave birth to the fire god in a burning thatched hut on Ōmuroyama.</ref>. Like Ōmuroyama, {{ill|Kumomi Sengen Shrine|ja|雲見浅間神社|qq=draft}} on Mount Eboshi (in {{ill|Matsuzaki, Shizuoka|ja|松崎町|qq=draft}}, Kamo District, Shizuoka Prefecture), an isolated peak with views of Mount Fuji, also enshrines the elder sister.
* '''Gochi Nyorai Jizō-son''' (五智如来地蔵尊)<ref name="gochi">Source: [On-site explanation board](http://obtweb.typepad.jp/.a/6a0120a877a494970b0192ac3acebb970d-800wi), accessed January 2016</ref> - Five statues of Buddha located on the western edge of the crater, built during the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代|qq=draft}}. Visitors come to pray for good relationships and safe childbirth. In 1663 (Kanbun 3), {{ill|Asakura Seibei|ja|朝倉清兵衛|qq=draft}}<ref name="odawaracity">Regarding Asakura Seibei, the on-site explanation board (viewed in 2018) describes him as a fishing boss. As a reference, [Odawara City Historical Materials](https://www.city.odawara.kanagawa.jp/field/lifelong/property/cultural/rekishishiryou/rekishichoukouzan.html) (January 2011) mentions a stonemason of the same name from {{ill|Iwamura, Kanagawa|ja|岩村|qq=draft}} who worked on the construction of Shōtaiji Temple in Kanbun 9 (1669).</ref>, a fishing boss from Iwamura in {{ill|Sagami Province|ja|相模国|qq=draft}} (now {{ill|Manazuru, Kanagawa|ja|真鶴町|qq=draft}}, Kanagawa Prefecture), prayed at Ōmuroyama Sengen Shrine for the safe childbirth of his 9-year-old daughter. In gratitude for her safe delivery, he erected these statues of the {{ill|Five Wisdom Buddhas|ja|五智如来|qq=draft}}<ref>On the on-site explanation board (viewed in 2018), the title reads "Gochi Nyorai Jizō-son," and the explanatory text mentions the creation of "Gochi Nyorai-zō." However, since the stone statues resemble Buddhas rather than Jizō, and because Nyorai-zō refers to carved stone figures, "Five Wisdom Buddha Statues" is used here.</ref>. They are said to be made of {{ill|Manazuru stone|ja|真鶴石|qq=draft}} and were transported from {{ill|Futo Port|ja|富戸港|qq=draft}}.
* '''Yatsugatake Jizō-son''' (八ケ岳地蔵尊)<ref name="yatsu">Source: [On-site explanation board](http://canned-seachicken.up.n.seesaa.net/canned-seachicken/image/E585ABE383B6E5B2B3E59CB0E894B5E5B08AE381AEE794B1E69DA5.jpg), accessed January 2016</ref> - Eight {{ill|Jizō|ja|地蔵|qq=draft}} statues located on the southern edge of the crater. They were erected by fishermen who used Ōmuroyama as a landmark, praying for maritime safety and good catches. There are both old and new statues, with the front ones built in 1984 (Shōwa 59) through donations from local residents of {{ill|Ike District|ja|池区|qq=draft}}.
<gallery> File:Itoh_20181124_115026.jpg|link=https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E3%83%95%E3%82%A1%E3%82%A4%E3%83%AB:Itoh_20181124_115026.jpg|Five Wisdom Buddha Statues File:Itoh_20181124_113718.jpg|link=https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E3%83%95%E3%82%A1%E3%82%A4%E3%83%AB:Itoh_20181124_113718.jpg|Yatsugatake Jizō-son File:Ōmuroyama_Sengen_Jinja_Haiden_and_Sessha.jpg|link=https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E3%83%95%E3%82%A1%E3%82%A4%E3%83%AB:%C5%8Cmuroyama_Sengen_Jinja_Haiden_and_Sessha.jpg|Ōmuroyama Sengen Shrine </gallery>

File:Ōmuroyama Sengen Jinja Haiden and Donation box 2.jpg|alt=Close-up of the Haiden and donation box at Ōmuroyama Sengen Jinja.|Haiden and Donation Box - Closer perspective showcasing the architectural details. File:Ōmuroyama Sengen Jinja Haiden and Donation box 3.jpg|alt=Haiden and donation box at Ōmuroyama Sengen Jinja with intricate woodwork.|Haiden and Donation Box - Highlighting the intricate woodwork and spiritual ambiance. File:Ōmuroyama Sengen Jinja Haiden and Donation box 4.jpg|alt=Wide view of Haiden and donation box at Ōmuroyama Sengen Jinja.|Haiden and Donation Box - A wider view capturing the surrounding area of the shrine. File:Ōmuroyama Sengen Jinja Haiden and Donation box 5.jpg|alt=Haiden and donation box at Ōmuroyama Sengen Jinja in a bright setting.|Haiden and Donation Box - Bright lighting emphasizing the vibrant environment. File:Ōmuroyama Sengen Jinja Haiden and Donation box 6.jpg|alt=Haiden and donation box at Ōmuroyama Sengen Jinja with serene surroundings.|Haiden and Donation Box - Another perspective with serene surroundings. File:Ōmuroyama Sengen Jinja Haiden stickers.jpg|alt=Stickers and inscriptions on the Haiden at Ōmuroyama Sengen Jinja.|Haiden Stickers - Close-up of decorative stickers and inscriptions adorning the Haiden. </gallery>
=== Goshuin === <gallery mode="packed"> File:Omuroyama Sengen Shrine Shuin self service stamps.jpg|alt=Self-service stamp station for Goshuin.|Shuin Stamps - A station for visitors to collect commemorative stamps. File:Omuroyama Sengen Shrine Shuin.jpg|alt=Sample Goshuin stamp from the shrine.|Goshuin - Beautiful calligraphy collected as a memento of the visit. </gallery>
=== Torii Gates === <gallery mode="packed"> File:Omuroyama Sengen Shrine Torii 1.jpg|alt=A torii gate leading to the shrine.|Torii - Traditional gate marking the entrance to sacred grounds. File:View of Omuroyama Torii from the bathroom above.jpg|alt=Unique view of the Torii from above.|Torii View - A unique perspective from higher ground. </gallery>

=== Ōmuroyama Sengen Jinja From Afar === <gallery mode="packed"> File:Crater of Mount Omuro (Izu Peninsula)-14.jpg|alt=View of Mount Omuro’s crater.|Crater View - Highlighting the natural beauty surrounding the shrine. File:Ōmuroyama Sengen Jinja sessha 2.jpg|alt=Front view of a sessha at Ōmuroyama Sengen Jinja.|Sessha - The front of the sessha, showcasing its traditional architectural design. {{distinguish|{{ill|Omuroyama Sengen Shrine|ja|大室山浅間神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Omuroyama Sengen Shrine|qq=}}|{{ill|Omuro Sengen Shrine (Ōasomi, Fujiyoshida)|ja|小室浅間神社_(富士吉田市大明見)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Omuro Sengen Shrine (Ōasomi, Fujiyoshida)|qq=}}|{{ill|Fuji Omuro Asama Shrine|ja|冨士御室浅間神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fuji Omuro Asama Shrine|qq=}}}}[[File:Mount_Omuroyama_Shrine_from_other_side_of_crater.jpg|thumb|View of the shrine that clearly shows its position in the crater]][[File:Ōmuroyama Sengen Jinja Torii.jpg|thumb|the entrance torii]] Omuroyama Sengen Shrine (大室山浅間神社) is a Shrine for [[Iwanaga-hime]]. It is present on [[Mount Ōmuro (Shizuoka)|Mount Ōmuro]]
It has a wikimedia commons Category here [https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/Category%3A%C5%8Cmuroyama_Sengen_Jinja]
== Overview ==
Omuroyama Sengen Shrine is a Shinto shrine located within the crater of Mount Omuro in Ito City, Shizuoka Prefecture, Japan. Established in 1654 by Matsudaira Izumokami, the shrine is dedicated to the deities associated with safe childbirth and matchmaking.<ref>https://oneclick-onetrip-blog.com/en/shizuoka-prefecture-mt-omuro/</ref>
Visitors can reach the summit of Mount Omuro, standing at 580 meters above sea level, via a six-minute chairlift ride. From there, a steep staircase near the lift station leads down into the crater where the shrine is situated. The crater also features an archery range, offering a unique experience for visitors.<ref>https://oneclick-onetrip-blog.com/en/shizuoka-prefecture-mt-omuro/</ref>
The shrine is part of a broader tradition of Sengen shrines in Japan, which are typically dedicated to Konohanasakuya-hime, the deity associated with Mount Fuji and volcanoes. These shrines are often located near volcanic mountains and are linked to prayers for protection against eruptions and natural disasters.<ref>https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sengen_shrine</ref>
Mount Omuro itself is a prominent feature of the Izu Peninsula, known for its distinctive bowl-shaped silhouette and lush greenery. The mountain offers panoramic views of the surrounding area, including sights of Mount Fuji, the Izu Islands, and Sagami Bay on clear days. An annual event called "Yamayaki," where the entire mountain is set ablaze, takes place on the second Sunday of February, attracting numerous tourists.<ref>https://savorjp.info/scenery/nature/6097/</ref>
For travelers planning a visit, the area around Mount Omuro and Omuroyama Sengen Shrine offers various attractions, including the Izu Shaboten Zoo and scenic walking paths. Accommodations are available nearby, with several hotels and guesthouses catering to different preferences.<ref>https://www.tripadvisor.ca/HotelsNear-g298123-d10339157-Omuroyama_Sengen_Shrine-Ito_Shizuoka_Prefecture_Tokai_Chubu.html</ref>
Experiencing Omuroyama Sengen Shrine provides insight into Japan's rich cultural heritage and offers a serene environment for reflection amidst natural beauty.
== Mythology == Ōmuroyama Sengen Shrine is dedicated to Iwanaga hime, where she is worshipped through a [[Kannabi]] that represents her residing directly within the mountain as her [[shintai]].<ref name=":2">{{Cite web|title=パワースポット|url=https://omuroyama.com/event/powerspot/|access-date=2024-05-17|website=大室山登山リフトオフィシャルサイト}}</ref>
Omuroyama itself was once believed to be divine. Now houses Sengen Shrine on its mid slope near the crater.<ref name=":2" />
The shrine houses the deity Iwanaga hime no mikoto, known for her connection, to safe childbirth and matchmaking. She is the sister of Konohanasakuya hime the goddess revered at the peak of Mount Fuji.<ref name=":2" />
Located on the summit along a mountain path are five life sized Wisdom Buddhas (Gochi Nyorai). 300 years ago a nine year old girl, daughter of Asakura Seibee, an official from Iwamura in Sagami Province (now Ashigarakami District, Kanagawa Prefecture) had a safe delivery thanks to prayers offered at Omuroyama Sengen Shrine. In gratitude for this blessing these Buddhas were enshrined.<ref name=":2" />
In times local fishermen sought protection and safety from sea disasters, at Omuroyama facing Oshima Island. This led to the dedication of eight Jizo statues known as Yatsugatake Jizo at the summit of Omuroyama.<ref name=":2" />
At Omuroyama stands the haiku monument honoring Takaha Shugyo, who admired and cherished the Izu region alongside his master Akimoto Fujio.<ref name=":2" />
The haiku says, "Izu is where the sun gently falls, blooming with mandarin flowers " beautifully capturing the beauty of Izus landscape in the summer.<ref name=":2" />
== Shrines & Stone Buddhas ==
* '''Ōmuroyama Sengen Shrine''' (大室山浅間神社)<ref name="asama">Source: [On-site explanation board](http://itospa.com/sanpo/wp-content/uploads/2010/10/omuroyama101027-19.jpg), accessed January 2016</ref> - A shrine located on the eastern mid-slope within the crater. It enshrines {{ill|Iwanaga-hime no Mikoto|ja|磐長姫命|1=Iwanaga-hime|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwanaga-hime no Mikoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwanaga-hime|qq=}} as its sole deity. She is worshipped as a goddess of safe childbirth and matchmaking. While most {{ill|Sengen Shrines|ja|浅間神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengen Shrines|qq=}} primarily enshrine {{ill|Asama no Ōkami|ja|浅間大神|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asama no Ōkami|qq=}} and {{ill|Konohanasakuya-hime|ja|木花之佐久夜毘売|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Konohanasakuya-hime|qq=}}, it is rare to find one that venerates Iwanaga-hime no Mikoto<ref name="Arakawa2008">{{Cite journal|Japanese|author=Rie Arakawa|title=The Spiritual Power of Ishinaga-hime|journal=Gakushuin University Studies in Ancient Literature|volume=33|date=March 31, 2008|pages=1-26|issn=0916-2453}}</ref>. Iwanaga-hime, the elder sister, and Konohanasakuya-hime, the younger sister, were both married to {{ill|Ninigi no Mikoto|ja|瓊々杵命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninigi no Mikoto|qq=}}, but the elder sister, considered unattractive, was sent back<ref>In the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kojiki|qq=}}, Konohanasakuya-hime gives birth to the fire god in a burning birthing hut. However, according to the on-site explanation board (viewed in 2018), it is said that Iwanaga-hime, who was sent back, gave birth to the fire god in a burning thatched hut on Ōmuroyama.</ref>. Like Ōmuroyama, {{ill|Kumomi Sengen Shrine|ja|雲見浅間神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumomi Sengen Shrine|qq=}} on Mount Eboshi (in {{ill|Matsuzaki, Shizuoka|ja|松崎町|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Matsuzaki, Shizuoka|qq=}}, Kamo District, Shizuoka Prefecture), an isolated peak with views of Mount Fuji, also enshrines the elder sister.
* '''Gochi Nyorai Jizō-son''' (五智如来地蔵尊)<ref name="gochi">Source: [On-site explanation board](http://obtweb.typepad.jp/.a/6a0120a877a494970b0192ac3acebb970d-800wi), accessed January 2016</ref> - Five statues of Buddha located on the western edge of the crater, built during the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Edo period|qq=}}. Visitors come to pray for good relationships and safe childbirth. In 1663 (Kanbun 3), {{ill|Asakura Seibei|ja|朝倉清兵衛|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asakura Seibei|qq=}}<ref name="odawaracity">Regarding Asakura Seibei, the on-site explanation board (viewed in 2018) describes him as a fishing boss. As a reference, [Odawara City Historical Materials](https://www.city.odawara.kanagawa.jp/field/lifelong/property/cultural/rekishishiryou/rekishichoukouzan.html) (January 2011) mentions a stonemason of the same name from {{ill|Iwamura, Kanagawa|ja|岩村|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwamura, Kanagawa|qq=}} who worked on the construction of Shōtaiji Temple in Kanbun 9 (1669).</ref>, a fishing boss from Iwamura in {{ill|Sagami Province|ja|相模国|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sagami Province|qq=}} (now {{ill|Manazuru, Kanagawa|ja|真鶴町|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Manazuru, Kanagawa|qq=}}, Kanagawa Prefecture), prayed at Ōmuroyama Sengen Shrine for the safe childbirth of his 9-year-old daughter. In gratitude for her safe delivery, he erected these statues of the {{ill|Five Wisdom Buddhas|ja|五智如来|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Five Wisdom Buddhas|qq=}}<ref>On the on-site explanation board (viewed in 2018), the title reads "Gochi Nyorai Jizō-son," and the explanatory text mentions the creation of "Gochi Nyorai-zō." However, since the stone statues resemble Buddhas rather than Jizō, and because Nyorai-zō refers to carved stone figures, "Five Wisdom Buddha Statues" is used here.</ref>. They are said to be made of {{ill|Manazuru stone|ja|真鶴石|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Manazuru stone|qq=}} and were transported from {{ill|Futo Port|ja|富戸港|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Futo Port|qq=}}.
* '''Yatsugatake Jizō-son''' (八ケ岳地蔵尊)<ref name="yatsu">Source: [On-site explanation board](http://canned-seachicken.up.n.seesaa.net/canned-seachicken/image/E585ABE383B6E5B2B3E59CB0E894B5E5B08AE381AEE794B1E69DA5.jpg), accessed January 2016</ref> - Eight {{ill|Jizō|ja|地蔵|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jizō|qq=}} statues located on the southern edge of the crater. They were erected by fishermen who used Ōmuroyama as a landmark, praying for maritime safety and good catches. There are both old and new statues, with the front ones built in 1984 (Shōwa 59) through donations from local residents of {{ill|Ike District|ja|池区|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ike District|qq=}}.
<gallery> File:Itoh_20181124_115026.jpg|link=https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E3%83%95%E3%82%A1%E3%82%A4%E3%83%AB:Itoh_20181124_115026.jpg|Five Wisdom Buddha Statues File:Itoh_20181124_113718.jpg|link=https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E3%83%95%E3%82%A1%E3%82%A4%E3%83%AB:Itoh_20181124_113718.jpg|Yatsugatake Jizō-son File:Ōmuroyama_Sengen_Jinja_Haiden_and_Sessha.jpg|link=https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E3%83%95%E3%82%A1%E3%82%A4%E3%83%AB:%C5%8Cmuroyama_Sengen_Jinja_Haiden_and_Sessha.jpg|Ōmuroyama Sengen Shrine </gallery>
